information associated with your Hutchison Three Mobile account so they can reach out if needed. **Step 2: Review Your Contractual Agreement** Familiarize yourself with your service agreement or contract terms to understand what services or products should typically appear on your bill. If there are discrepancies between billed items and those outlined in your contract, note them down for reference during the dispute process. _Guideline:_ Keep an open mind while reviewing; sometimes misunderstandings occur due to unfamiliar jargon or unclear invoice descriptions. **Step 3: Contact Customer Service** Reach out to Hutchison Three Mobile's customer service department via their official channels - telephone, email, live chat, etc. Explain the situation clearly, providing all necessary details gathered in Step 1. Be patient and persistent until you receive satisfactory answers. _Key Tip:_ Maintain professionalism throughout your interaction; being rude or aggressive may hinder progress towards resolving your issue. **Step 4: Request Written Confirmation** Ask the representative to send written confirmation of their response regarding the disputed charges. Having everything in writing serves as evidence supporting your claim and protects both parties involved. _Guideline:_ Follow up politely if you haven't received the confirmation within a reasonable timeframe (usually one week). **Step 5: Escalate if Necessary** If the initial conversation doesn't yield favorable results, request to speak with a supervisor or manager who has more authority to make decisions about bill adjustments. Again, provide all pertinent information and remain calm and respectful. _Key Tip:_ Remember, employees at every level are people too! They want to assist you, so give them the opportunity to do so before escalating further. **Step 6: File a Formal Complaint** Should multiple attempts prove fruitless, consider filing a formal complaint through authorized regulatory bodies overseeing telecommunications companies in your region. Provide thorough documentation backing your case. _Guideline:_ Research these organizations ahead of time so you know where to direct your complaint and familiarize yourself with any specific procedures required. **Step 7: Seek Legal Advice (Optional)** As a last resort, consult with a legal expert specializing in consumer rights. While expensive, litigation might become necessary if substantial financial loss has occurred due to erroneous bills. _Key Tip:_ Always try amicable resolution methods first before pursuing legal action. Lawsuits should generally be considered a final measure when other avenues fail. By following these steps meticulously, you increase your chances of successfully disputing unwarranted Hutchison Three Mobile charges. Stay proactive, maintain clear communication, and keep records of all interactions related to your dispute. [much time passes as MOT watches PLA] My mom would never let me eat all that junk food. " Mary's got him socks all wet. " Title: How to Add Cardio to Your Workout Cardiovascular exercise, or cardio, is an essential component of any well-rounded fitness routine. It improves heart health, increases stamina, and aids in weight management. This step-by-step guide will help you incorporate cardio into your workout regimen effectively and safely. **Step 1: Consult with Your Doctor** Before starting any new exercise program, including cardio, consult with your healthcare provider. They can provide personalized advice based on your current health status and medical history. *Key Tip*: Honesty about your fitness level and existing conditions ensures safe recommendations from your doctor. **Step 2: Identify Your Fitness Goals** Determine what you want to achieve through adding cardio to your workout. Common goals include losing weight, improving endurance, or preparing for a specific event like a race. *Guideline*: Clear objectives make it easier to choose appropriate exercises and intensity levels. **Step 3: Select Suitable Activities** Choose activities that suit your interests, lifestyle, and environment. Some popular forms of cardio are running, swimming, cycling, dancing, jumping rope, and using elliptical machines. *Key Tip*: Variety keeps workouts interesting and prevents plateaus in progress. **Step 4: Establish a Schedule** Decide how many days per week you'll do cardio. The American Heart Association recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity or 75 minutes of vigorous activity weekly. *Guideline*: Spread out sessions throughout the week rather than doing them all at once. **Step 5: Begin Gradually** Start slowly, especially if you're new to regular exercise. Gradual increase in duration and intensity reduces risk of injury and soreness. *Key Tip*: Aim for 20-30 minute sessions initially, then add time as fitness improves. **Step 6: Monitor Intensity** Use the Talk Test, Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE), or target heart rate zones to gauge whether your workout is light, moderate, or vigorous. *Guideline*: During moderately intense exercise, you should be able to talk but not sing; during vigorous exercise, conversation becomes difficult. **Step 7: Cross Train** Incorporate different types of cardio into your routine to avoid overuse injuries and improve overall fitness. *Key Tip*: For example, alternate between running, spinning, and swimming. **Step 8: Stay Hydrated** Drink plenty of water before, during, and after workouts to replace fluids lost through sweat. *Guideline*: Thirst is not always a reliable indicator of hydration needs. **Step 9: Listen to Your Body** Pay attention to signs of fatigue, pain, or excessive shortness of breath. These could indicate overexertion or potential health issues. *Key Tip*: Rest when needed and seek medical advice if symptoms persist. **Step 10: Track Progress** Keep records of distance covered, speed, calories burned, etc., to see improvements over time. *Guideline*: Apps, fitness trackers, or old-fashioned paper logs can serve this purpose. Remember, consistency is crucial when adding cardio to your workout. Regular engagement leads to noticeable changes in energy levels, body composition, and overall health. Happy exercising! The 'image dances', according to Sachs, are to do with the world outside the dancer. By imitating an animal or object, the dancer believes he can capture a power and make it useful. To dance in imitation of the animal which is going to be hunted is to become one with them. To imitate the act of sex is to achieve fertility. This is the kind of thinking behind an image dance. Sachs points out that societies of this kind do not really understand the connection between cause and effect. They really believe the image dances work. In this crafty lesson plan, students make their own insects to learn about the physical characteristics and anatomy of insects. - Grades: PreK-K - Unit Plan: Students explore the world of insects and discover what it might be like to live like one. They create an insect with physical characteristics and demonstrate how it moves. They also compare insects to spiders. - Examine the physical characteristics of insects: six legs, three body parts, two wings (for some), two eyes, two antennae, skeleton on the outside. - Contrast insects and spiders. - Compare how different insects move: jump, fly, crawl, or swim. - Enough insect photographs for each child, plus a couple of spider pictures - Magnifying glasses for each student - Various live (or dead) insects from pet stores or educational supply stores - Paper egg cartons - Pipe cleaners (3 of one color; 1 of another) per student - 2" x 2" pieces of cardstock for each student - Foil wrap - Colorful markers - Clear tape - Hole punch - Multiple colors of tempera paint - Paint brushes - Paper plates for each student - Chart paper - Four hula hoops or rings made of loose string - Four blank word cards Set Up and Prepare - Preview Going Bug-gy! for photo ideas and insect information. - Cut paper egg cartons into segments of three cups in a row (one per student) - Cut a 2" x 2" piece of cardstock for each student. - Cut a 6" x 6" piece of foil for each student. - Cut and hide pictures of insects and a few spiders throughout the classroom. - Cover the desks with newspapers and set out various colors of paint for Day 2. - Write or label student names on the inside of the cut egg cartons. Step 1: Before the students come in, hide pictures (enough for each student to find one) of insects throughout the room. Set out a couple pictures of spiders as well. Suspend flying insects from the ceiling on strings. Step 2: If you have a live insect, gather the students in a circle on the carpet. Distribute the magnifying glasses. Tell them that they will be studying insects, bugs, and spiders and that bugs are insects with mouthparts that pierce and suck. Pass around the insect (in a jar or small plastic terrarium) for the students to examine. Step 3: Ask students to share what they know about insects. Tell them that all around the room are pictures of different kinds of insects. Invite them to find one picture and bring it back to the carpet. Step 4: When they're finished finding pictures, ask students to turn to a partner and tell about their creature. Encourage them to describe the colors and anything else they notice. Step 5: Explain that most of them have pictures of insects, but a few have spiders. Ask if anyone knows of any differences between insects and spiders. Write headings Insects and Spiders on chart paper and sketch each creature underneath the words. As you draw, explain the basic physical characteristics: Have 3 body parts: head, thorax and abdomen Have 2 eyes and 2 antennas that help the insect smell, taste, and hear Middle section (thorax) has powerful muscles that operate wings and six legs Back section (abdomen) expands when the insect feeds Have their skeletons on the outside. Only have 2 body parts: abdomen and cephalothorax (head and thorax together) Growing from the cephalothorax are 8 legs. Have 8 eyes and 2 pedipalps - leg-like body parts that touch and taste - instead of antennae Step 6: Ask students to look at their picture and tape it in the correct category on the chart. As they do, ask them if they know the name of the insect. If they don't, name the insects. Step 1: If you have another live insect to share, pass it around for students to examine with their magnifying glasses. Ask them to recall the different colors, spots, and stripes they may have noticed on the live insects, the pictures, or ones they've seen before. Tell students that today they are going to create their own insect. Distribute the three-cupped egg cartons. Ask students to think about what kind of insect they want to make. Invite them to look at the pictures on the chart. What color will they make their insects? Will they have stripes or spots? Will they have wings? Step 2: Send students back to their seats and invite them to paint the three body parts of their insect any way they like. Leave time for paint to dry. Step 3: Gather students together and ask them to share the kind of insect they're creating and what features they need to add. Step 1: Before students arrive, punch three holes on each side of the center egg carton cup (thorax) and two on either side of the head. Set out three pipe cleaners, 2" x 2" piece of cardstock, and a 6" x 6" inch piece of foil on a paper plate for each student. Set out colored markers, scissors, and glue. Step 2: Gather students together and ask them to recall the physical characteristics of insects you discussed. Tell them that the holes punched in each insect's thorax will help them create legs by threading the pipe cleaners through them. They can create wings by coloring and cutting them out of foil and gluing them on the thorax. Finally, they can thread another pipe cleaner through the head holes for antennas. They can also draw, cut out, and glue on eyes. Step 3: Let students create their insects. Let glue dry. Step 4: Close the lesson by sharing a book about insects. See my booklist for suggestions. Step 1: Ask students how people move. Accept all answers. Refer them to the pictures on the Insects and Spiders chart from Day 1. Explain that insects move in many ways. Step 2: Write the following words on word cards with the students: fly, crawl, jump, and swim. Lay four hula hoops on the classroom floor (or create four rings of string) and place a word card in each. Step 3: Ask students to take one of the insects from the chart and classify how they think that insect might move: fly, crawl, jump, or swim. Assist students where needed. Step 4: Distribute the students' insects. Go outdoors and invite students to move their insect in the way they think it might move. Supporting All Learners Where appropriate, help students to categorize their insect. Assist students who might have difficulty cutting with scissors. I place a piece of black construction paper in a large copy paper lid, and the students roll a marble coated with white paint. The trail of the marble creates a web painting on which the spider is mounted. Encourage students to search for insects in their home or outside with their family. Ask them to collect some in a plastic container to share with the class or draw a picture of it. Title: How to Change the Font Type Using HTML Programming HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language used to create web pages. It allows you to format text, create links, add images, and structure content on your website. One simple yet impactful way to customize the look of your webpage using HTML is by changing the font type. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step. **Step 1:** Understand Basic HTML Structure Before diving into specifics, familiarize yourself with basic HTML syntax. A typical HTML document consists of various elements enclosed within tags. Tags start with `<` and end with `>`. Most tags come in pairs - an opening tag and a closing tag. The content goes between these two tags. For example: ```html <p>This is a paragraph.</p> ``` In this case, `<p>` is the opening tag for a paragraph while `</p>` serves as its closing tag. Key Tip: Always close your tags unless they are self-closing like `<img />`, which we'll see later. **Step 2:** Choose Your Desired Font There are thousands of fonts available online; some free, others paid. Popular sources include Google Fonts ([https://fonts.google.com](https://fonts.google.com)), Font Squirrel ([www.fontsquirrel.com](http://www.fontsquirrel.com)), etc. Download or link to your chosen font. We're using 'Roboto' from Google Fonts for this tutorial. **Step 3:** Link the Font in Your HTML Document To use external fonts, you need to import them via CSS (Cascading Style Sheets). Add the following line within the `<head></head>` section of your HTML file: ```html <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto&display=swap" rel="stylesheet"> I noticed that she has a bunch of clean up routines. Moose are large even-toed herbivorous mammals, the largest of the deer family. Moose vary in size and shape. Their colour varies from a little brown to a dusty black depending on the season and age of the animal. Calves, in comparison, are often a light rusty colour. Moose have long, lighter-colored legs with the front pair longer than the hind ones. Other features include a long nose, drooping lip, hump at the shoulders and small tail. The flap of skin that hangs beneath the throat is called a BELL. Males weigh on average over 550 kg (1200 lbs) and females often more than 400 kg (900 lbs). New born calves weigh around 15 kg but quickly increase in size, around 300 or 400 pounds by its first winter. An Alaskan moose, one of the largest sub-group, discovered in 1897 holds the record for being the largest known modern deer. It was a bull standing 2.34 metres and weighed 816kg. Its RACK (or antler spread) was 199cm. Height at the shoulders generally ranges between 6 -7 feet (over 2 metres). The other end of the size scale is the smaller Shiras moose, also known as the 'Wyoming' or 'Yellowstone' moose. These animals are lighter in colour around the ears and back and have smaller hooves and antlers. Unlike other subspecies they are comfortable at higher elevations. A young moose's first pair of antlers grow from two tiny bumps on their head that they have had from birth. A bull of about a year old can be seen on the right with the first signs of antler growth visible. Mature males shed their antlers once a year in November/December and replace them with new larger ones in the Spring. They are formed of living tissue supplied by blood through a network of vessels covered with a soft smooth skin called VELVET. Eventually the tissue solidifies, the velvet is scraped off and the antlers become completely formed of mineralised dead matter. Their main function is for display during the mating season and dominance within the herd. The reindeer/caribou is the only deer species in which both the male and female have antlers. The image on the right shows a replica female moose skull. The skull length is 63cm or 24.5 inches. A moose's teeth are specially designed for eating plant materials and for browsing on bushes and small trees. In all they have 32 teeth made up of 12 ridged molars, 12 premolars, 6 incisors and 2 canines: ... or return Home. " "No," replied the doctor. "I didn't come to complain, Hardy, I came to see if I could collect the mail for Santa Claus. " Mr. Hardy put down his pen, and stared at his visitor. "What are you driving at, anyhow?" he asked. Jill was very excited to see all the plants and asked if she could help him. The neighbor said, "Hm, I don't think so, it's a very tricky and hard job." Jill felt sad and thought it wasn't fair. She asked the neighbor why she couldn't help and the neighbor said, "It's too hard for someone your size." Jill was very angry and said, "I'm big enough!" She wanted to help her jolly neighbor so she tried her very best to plant the seeds, even if it was hard. Eventually, she managed to plant all the seeds and was so proud of what she had achieved. The neighbor smiled and said, "You did a great job, thank you very much!" Jill was very happy. She had noticed a difficult problem and found a way to help her jolly neighbor. The Metropolitan Line was the first part of the Underground to be made. It was opened in 1863. It then ran between Paddington and Farringdon. It took 40,000 passengers per day. Later it was made longer. The District Line was built by a different company. In 1884, the Circle Line was finished. All these lines used steam engines at first. Its ions are essential to all living cells. The ions play a major role in manipulating important biological polyphosphate compounds like ATP, DNA, and RNA. Hundreds of enzymes thus require magnesium ions to function. Magnesium is also the metallic ion at the center of chlorophyll, and is thus a common additive to fertilizers. Magnesium ions are sour to the taste, and in low concentrations help to impart a natural tartness to fresh mineral waters. 'Associative' refers to the grouping of numbers. The associative property of addition implies that, when adding three or more terms, it doesn't matter how these terms are grouped. Algebraically, this gives formula_11. Note that this does not hold for subtraction, e.g. formula_12 (see the distributive property). I wish you would bring it out here and walk up and down with it. Thoughts for Lessons: - This craft has lots of simple shapes to cut out -- good scissor practice! - You can precut the template into separate pieces (roughly) to make it a bit easier for beginning crafters to cut out the shapes. - show the children two stars and ask -- is this one bigger, smaller or the same size as that one. - Count the circles and the stars. - Print out the template. - If using the B&W template, colour the pieces. - Cut out the template pieces. - Glue the head (smaller star) onto the body (larger star). - Glue the moon shaped mouth onto the head. - Glue three eyes onto the head. - Glue the tummy eyeball onto the center of the body star. - Close the template window after printing to return to this screen. - Set page margins to zero if you have trouble fitting the template on one page (FILE, PAGE SETUP or FILE, PRINTER SETUP in most browsers). The term robber baron was first used in the 12th and 13th centuries to describe rich men who lived in large castles along major transportation rivers, like the Rhine in Europe. They would send ships out onto these rivers to stop anybody who was traveling through who did not know better and collect a tax from them on any money or goods on these ships. They were not officially allowed to collect these taxes. It was only the Holy Roman Emperor who could decide what taxes to be charged, how much and who could charge it. One day, a fox was wandering through the forest. The fox moved quickly and gracefully, her playful spirit showing in her every step. All of a sudden, she saw a small rabbit ahead. The rabbit immediately seemed scared and ran away. The fox was surprised, for she had no intention of scaring away the rabbit. She wanted to make friends! The fox chased after the rabbit and called out, "Don't be scared! I just want to be your friend." The rabbit stopped and looked back. It crept back slowly, not sure if it could trust the fox. The fox declared firmly, "I won't ever hurt you. I promise!" The rabbit hesitated, but felt that it could trust the fox. They became friends and played together all day. The moral of the story is that if you are kind and trustworthy, you will make good friends. And really if I know about children's authors I would keep on writing and writing him. The transmission, having two forward gears and one reverse, was of the planetary type, controlled by foot pedals rather than the more common hand lever used in sliding-gear transmissions. Spark and throttle were controlled by a hand lever on the steering column. The ten gallon fuel tank was located under the front seat. Because gasoline was fed to the engine only by gravity, and also because the reverse gear offered more power than the forward gears, the Model T frequently had to be driven up a steep hill backward. Such deficiencies, along with its homely appearance, less-than-comfortable ride at top speeds, and incessant rattling, made the Model T the butt of much affectionate humor in innumerable jokes, songs, poems, and stories. Caucasoid was a word for a person from Europe, West Asia, South Asia, Central Asia, North Africa, or the Horn of Africa. The group of these persons was called "Caucasoid race" or Caucasian race. In former times, many people divided human beings into three races. These races were called "Caucasoid", "Mongoloid", and "Negroid". Today, scientists agree that there is only one human race. Modern genetic research has shown that the idea of three races was wrong. Let me see the dog let me see the dog. December fifteenth two thousand nine seven month visit. "The Lord of the Rings" is often called a trilogy, because the publisher split the book into three parts because of the book's size. Tolkien himself had sub-divided "The Lord of the Rings" into six parts, called Book I-VI, according to the plot. Tolkien never liked it being published in three parts or called a trilogy. Disputing a background check can be a daunting task, but with careful planning and attention to detail, you can ensure that any errors or inaccuracies are corrected. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to dispute a background check, complete with explanations, key tips, and best practices. Step 1: Obtain a Copy of Your Background Report Before disputing a background check, obtain a copy of your report from the company that provided it. You have the right to request one free copy per year under the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA). Visit annualcreditreport.com to get started. Review the report thoroughly for any incorrect information, such as employment history, education, criminal records, credit reports, or personal identifying details. Make note of all discrepancies. Key Tip: Keep track of dates, names, contact information, and specific issues related to the errors found during this process. Documentation is crucial when dealing with consumer reporting agencies. Step 2: Identify the Consumer Reporting Agency (CRA) Determine which CRA supplied the inaccurate background check. Common CRAs include Equifax, Experian, TransUnion, and LexisNexis Risk Solutions. The CRA's name should be listed on the report along with their contact information. Step 3: Gather Supporting Documents Compile documents that support your claim, including proof of address, pay stubs, diplomas, court records, etc. These items help validate your identity and demonstrate why certain entries need correction. Be sure to photocopy these documents, as originals may not be returned by the CRA. Step 4: Initiate a Dispute Contact the CRA using their preferred method - typically via mail, phone, or online form. Clearly state that you wish to dispute inaccurate information and specify what needs correcting. Provide copies of supporting documentation, your full name, current address, date of birth, Social Security number, and other requested identification data. Send correspondence through certified mail with return receipt requested to confirm delivery. Key Guideline: Maintain professionalism throughout the dispute process. Being courteous and patient increases the likelihood of achieving a favorable resolution. Step 5: Wait for Investigation Results The FCRA mandates that CRAs investigate disputes within 30 days upon receiving them unless they consider your dispute frivolous. During this time, the CRA must also forward your complaint to the provider of the contested information, who then has an opportunity to respond. Expect communication regarding updates or requests for additional information from both parties involved. Step 6: Receive Outcome Notification Once the investigation concludes, the CRA must notify you in writing about its findings and supply another free copy of your updated report if changes were made. If the disputed item remains unchanged, you still have options. Step 7: File a Complaint with Federal Trade Commission (FTC) If the CRA fails to rectify the error after investigating, file a complaint with the FTC at ftccomplaintassistant.gov. While the FTC cannot resolve individual cases, collecting complaints allows them to monitor industry trends and enforce regulations. Step 8: Seek Legal Counsel (Optional) In some instances where significant harm has occurred due to inaccurate background checks, consulting with a lawyer specializing in consumer protection law might be necessary. They can offer guidance on potential legal remedies available under federal and state laws. Remember, persistence pays off when disputing background checks. Stay diligent in following up on communications, organizing documentation, and maintaining professionalism until satisfactory results are achieved. Once upon a time, there was a bird called Joe. Joe was a very obedient bird, who always listened to his mom. One day, Joe asked his mom, "Can I fly really high today?" His mom said, "Yes, go ahead." So Joe flew high and higher. He was so happy, and he kept flying. Soon, he was so far away! When Joe stopped flying, he looked around and he noticed he didn't recognise anything. He wasn't sure where he was. Then, out of the corner of his eye, he saw another bird. "What's your name?" Joe asked. The other bird said, "My name is Blue. Where are you from?" Joe told Blue that he had flown far away. Blue said, "Let me show you the way back." So Blue and Joe flew together until they reached Joe's home. Joe thanked Blue and said goodbye. As Joe flew home, he was so happy he had such a good friend. He looked forward to their next adventure together. Learning to Draw is Fun and Educational for Kids (and adults!). It allows children to have fun while developing their fine motor skills and creative skills. As your child's fine motor skills will have improved over their fist year, they are probably physically able to hold a crayon at around 12 months and start drawing. If your child takes longer, that's fine too as children develop at different rates. Drawing is an important skill for your child to learn at an early age. It provides the basics for developing your child's creative skills along with their reading, writing and even mathematics. You should encourage your child to improve their skills, but remember that they should also have some free time with no restrictions to draw what they want. Your child's education is important, so it should be kept interesting, enjoyable, not forced. The lesson notes below should be used as guidelines that can inspire you, and help give you ideas of your own, to help your child learn to draw. 12 to 30 months: Start by getting some crayons, paper and explaining to your child that you want to draw with them. Start by drawing a line and getting your child to choose a color and continue drawing where you left off. Get your child to say the name of each color as they use it and you can do the same when it's your turn. Continue taking turns drawing straight and curved lines until the paper is full of designs and scribbles. You can now color in the shapes your lines have made and take note of any obvious shapes that may appear by saying them aloud. If your child at this stage is able to draw vertical or horizontal lines or even a circle you should praise them well. Drawing with a crayon involves grasping and holding along with other fine motor skills and will give your child's confidence a boost along with developing their vision and imagination. Always talk to your child about the picture that you are creating together. Once completed hang it on the refrigerator for you both to enjoy. You should note that premature babies may take longer to reach key milestones. Also, many kids don't start scribbling until they are 15 or 16 months old, and continue on to be excellent students at school. 31 to 36 months: By the time your child reaches two and a half, they will be able to hold a crayon well. Children at this age can now draw angels like the "V" shape which is more difficult than drawing straight lines. Your toddler will also have the ability to make circular shapes and maybe even able to write a few letters. If your child is not at this level don't feel pressured, wait until they are really interested and excited. Your toddler will draw in his or her own good time and will still be on track developmentally. Get your child to explain every squiggle in their drawing. It may look like a scribble to you but it could be of great relevance to them. At this age you will notice your toddler spending more time at their drawings as they are usually keen scribblers. Make sure you supervise as your walls and furniture can be subjected to crayon markings too. 3 to 4 years: You can now get your child to draw basic shapes e.g. circles, triangles and squares. First draw the shape your self and then get your child to copy it. Your child will soon learn how to draw each of these shapes by themselves. You can combine drawing with learning how to count by counting the corners of each shape. You child will enjoy coloring in the shapes that you have created together. At this stage many children will be able to draw several letters of the alphabet too. Children are usually keen on drawing people. Start your child with matchstick men and he/she will add on more details as they get better. How to quickly print these notes and guidelines: Use your mouse to highlight all of text that you want to print out. Then copy/paste that text into a text-only editor on your computer. For example, Notepad, a common text editor included with all versions of Microsoft Windows, and found in Start, All Programs, Accessories. Then click on "File" in the top left corner of the Notepad document, and click on "Print". - Drawing Lessons for Kids aged 5 - 10 years. Lesson 18 Section 4 RATIO AND PROPORTION 2 Example 1. Joan earns $1600 a month, and pays $400 in rent. Express that fact in the language of ratio. Answer. "A quarter of Joan's salary goes for rent." That sentence, or one like it, expresses the ratio of $400 to $1600, of the part that goes for rent to the whole. We are not concerned with the numbers themselves, but only their ratio. Example 2. In Jim's class there are 30 pupils, while in Jane's there are only 10. Express that fact in the language of ratio. Answer. "In Jim's class there are three times as many as in Jane's." This expresses the ratio of 30 pupils to 10. Example 3. In a class of 24 students, there were 16 B's. Express that fact in the language of ratio. Answer. "Two thirds of the class got B." This expresses the ratio of the part that got B to the whole number of students: 16 out of 24. Their common divisor is 8. 8 goes into 16 two times and into 24 three times. 16 is two thirds of 24. Example 10. This month's bill is $75, while last month's was only $30. Express that fact in the language of ratio. Answer. "This month's bill is two and a half times last month's." 75 is equal to two times 30 (60) with a remainder of 15, which is half of 30. 75 = 60 + 15. 75 is two and a half times 30. The student will hear this language, will read it, and should be able to understand it and speak it. At this point, please "turn" the page and do some Problems. Continue on to the next Lesson. 'Cause 'cause XXX give me a hug 'cause I was sad. Five Fun Ways to Form Small GroupsSmall group work can be an effective way for students to actively participate in their learning. Groups of two and three students work well for activities which deal with sensitive topics and for encouraging quiet students to participate. Larger groups of four or more are effective when the activity involves collecting a variety of input like list making and brainstorming. Once you have determined the small group size you want, divide the total group size by the small group size. This will give you the number of small groups you want. For example, if you have 30 students and you want them in groups of five, divide 30 by five to find out you want to create six groups. Whether you want to create pairs or groups of seven, here are some fun recommendations for you to try the next time you assign students to small groups. - Stack the Deck Playing cards is a great tool for creating groups of two, three and four. Prepare ahead of time by creating your own stacked deck of cards with one card for each student. Stack your deck with two, three or four of a kind cards, corresponding with the number of students per group, two, three or four. Make sure the deck is shuffled. Pass out cards to each student. Ask students to find the other students who have the same numbered/face card. Using playing cards to form small groups is particularly helpful when you want to have students work in pairs and then combine the pairs to create groups of four. With playing cards, you can create groups of two (grouped by like numbered/face cards of the same color) and have them combine into groups of four (grouped by like numbered/face cards of any color). Names of popular singers and musical groups can be used to create groups of any size. Once you have determined how many students will make up each small group, generate a list of that many musicians. Back Street Boys, Brandi, Jennifer Lopez, Ricky Martin, N'Sync, Will Smith, Brittney Spears are possible choices, and you can solicit more ideas from your students. Prepare strips of paper with the musicians' names on them. Hand the slips of paper out randomly to the students and ask them to create groups of like musicians. For some added fun, encourage students to sing a song (or hum a tune) from their assigned musician while they seek their other small group members. - Wish You Were Here Postcards. If you want to create groups of two, postcards are great fun. Prepare by collecting a set of postcards which all have unique pictures on them. Cut the postcards in halves. Pass out the postcard halves to each student. Ask students to pair up with the students with the other half of their postcard. - Talk Show Hosts Names of talk show hosts can be used to create groups of any size. You might want to start out by asking the students in advance which talk shows they watch most often and using the names of those hosts. Or you can simply choose the most popular talk show hosts such as: Oprah, Ellen, Jerry Springer, Jay Leno, Conan, and David Letterman. Decide on how many students you want in each group and create that number of 3 x 5 index cards for each talk show host. For example, if you want four students in each group, you will need to have four index cards with Oprah's name on them, etc. Mix the cards up and distribute them randomly to the students. Then ask the students to find the others with the same index cards. - Birthday Line-Up The birthday line-up can be used to create groups of any size and does not require any materials or preparation. This grouping process may take a bit longer than the others but it is a good team building exercise in itself. Ask students to form a line in chronological order of their birthdays. Once in a line, count off the appropriate number of students into groups. For example, if forming triads, inform the first three people in line that they are a group, and continue until all students are grouped. For skill building information on managing small group activities, check out Managing Small Groups in ReCAPP's Educator Skills section. One example, which has lasted, was his reform of the calendar into the present format, with a leap day every four years. [ROM takes a shape from the page] Title: How to Be the Perfect Daughter Introduction: Being the perfect daughter can be a challenging yet rewarding experience. It requires effort, dedication, and a strong commitment to building a healthy relationship with your parents and family members. This guide will provide you with actionable steps and valuable insights that will help you become the ideal daughter while maintaining your individuality and personal growth. Step 1: Communicate Openly and Honestly * Explanation: Effective communication forms the foundation of any successful relationship. By openly discussing your thoughts, feelings, and concerns with your parents, you demonstrate respect, trust, and understanding. * Tips: + Practice active listening by focusing on what your parents are saying without interrupting them. + Show empathy towards their perspective and validate their emotions. + Express gratitude and appreciation for their efforts and sacrifices. + Avoid using accusatory language; instead, use "I" statements to share your own experiences and viewpoints. Step 2: Respect Your Parents' Authority and Rules * Explanation: Upholding your parents' rules and expectations shows that you value their guidance and wisdom. This fosters mutual respect and strengthens familial bonds. * Guidelines: + Understand and adhere to house rules regarding curfews, chores, and other responsibilities. + Recognize that your parents have your best interests at heart, even if you don't always agree with their decisions. Title: How to Get Your Children to Make Their Beds Consistently Introduction: Making one's bed is a simple task that can instill responsibility, discipline, and time management skills in children from a young age. This habit also promotes orderliness and contributes to maintaining a tidy home environment. Here are some steps, along with their rationale and helpful suggestions, to encourage your kids to make their beds consistently. Step 1: Set Clear Expectations Explain to your child why making their bed matters. Connect this chore to concepts like pride in ownership, personal space organization, or being part of a team (family) effort to maintain a clean household. Emphasize that consistency is crucial; they should aim to make their bed every morning after waking up. Key Tip: Be patient and persistent in communicating these ideas. It may take several conversations before the message resonates with them. Step 2: Break Down the Task Demonstrate how to make the bed properly by dividing it into smaller tasks such as smoothing out the sheets, arranging pillows, tucking in blankets, etc. By breaking down the process, you help your child understand what needs to be done, reducing feelings of overwhelm and increasing likelihood of success. Guideline: Consider creating a checklist detailing each sub-task so they have visual cues to guide them through the routine. Step 3: Establish a Reward System Children often respond well to positive reinforcement. Develop a rewards system based on consistent behavior rather than immediate perfection. For instance, if they successfully make their bed for five consecutive days, treat them to something small like extra playtime or their favorite meal. Key Tip: Avoid using punishment as motivation. Instead, focus on celebrating achievements to foster intrinsic motivation. Step 4: Create a Morning Schedule Incorporate bed-making into a larger morning schedule which includes activities like brushing teeth, washing face, getting dressed, etc. Having a structured sequence of events helps create structure and familiarity, making it easier for children to remember and execute their responsibilities. Guideline: Use visual schedules with pictures or symbols for younger kids who cannot read yet. Step 5: Praise Effort Over Outcome Praising the act of trying instead of just the end result encourages persistence and resilience. Acknowledge their efforts even when the final product isn't perfect - remember, we're focusing on building habits here! Step 6: Lead by Example Let your child see you making your own bed regularly. Kids tend to mimic adult behaviors, so leading by example could significantly influence their inclination towards adopting this new habit. Step 7: Gradually Increase Responsibility As your child becomes more comfortable with making their bed, gradually introduce additional responsibilities related to bedroom maintenance, such as putting away toys or clothes. Remember to acknowledge their progress and celebrate milestones along the way. Conclusion: Establishing a consistent bed-making habit takes time and patience. Stay committed to guiding your child through this learning journey while keeping communication open and supportive. With steady practice and encouragement, making beds will become second nature to both you and your child, fostering an atmosphere of organization and accountability within your home. [places a silver man on the base next to the orange ball] Acids and bases typically exist together in equilibrium. This means that within a sample of an acid, some molecules will give up their protons and others will accept them. Even water is a mixture of an acidic ion, HO (called a hydronium ion) and a basic ion, OH (called a hydroxide ion). A hydronium ion will give up its proton to a hydroxide ion, forming two molecules of HO, which is neutral. This reaction happens continuously in a sample of water, but overall the sample is neutral because there are equal amounts of hydronium and hydroxide in the sample. For most reactions, however, the acids and bases are not present in equal amounts, and this imbalance is what allows a chemical reaction to occur. Hey do you want some mashed potatoes? [ROM struggles from MOT to turn around] [looking at book and talking not very clearly] Rational: Young children need to develop the ability to ask questions. Asking questions while reading will improve a child s reading comprehension and memory. Materials: The Borrowers by Mary Norton, Random House, 1988, paper, pencils, chalk and chalkboard, A New Day (multiple copies), Silver Burdett and Ginn, 1989 1. " Today class, we are going to learn a strategy to help us become better readers." "The strategy is called question generation." 2. " Before we begin, I want to review something we learned last week." " Remember silent reading is when you read to yourself without moving your lips." I will demonstrate this to the children to activate their prior knowledge. " I want you to practice your silent reading by reading the back cover of the borrowers." "Good, lets move on." 3."Ok, lets talk about asking questions." "I have a book called The Borrowers and I am going to read what it is about on the back side." "As I am reading, I am going to ask out loud some questions that come to my mind." [Teacher reads the following: Only pod is allowed to venture into the house above, because the danger being seen by a human is too great.] "What is a pod? Why is it too dangerous? Why can t they be seen by a human?" "I read only a few sentences and I have so many questions." It is important when you ask questions that they cover important information and bring the story together.All your questions should be answered.My questions are important because it is information I do not know and it is important to the story. "This skill is so important because it will help you understand better what you are reading and it will improve your memory." "I want you to try it." [Talking to the whole class] "I want you to read the first page of the Borrowers, come up with some questions and then raise your hand." The teacher will write the questions on the board when they raise their hand. "Okay, great questions!" Those questions were great because they are bringing the story together. 4. For practice I would have the students read the next page and write their question down on a piece of paper. I would have them pair up and ask each other the questions. 5.Assessment:Students will read a passage from A New Day and make up a quiz. Reference: Pressley, Michael. What are you doin' over there little girl? The nucleus is in the middle of an atom. It is made up of protons and neutrons. Usually in nature, two things with the same charge repel or shoot away from each other. So for a long time it was a mystery to scientists how the positively charged protons in the nucleus stayed together. They solved this by finding a particle called a "gluon". Its name comes from the word "glue" as gluons act like atomic glue, sticking the protons together using the "strong nuclear force". It is this force which also holds the quarks together that make up the protons and neutrons. [MOT and Chi remove all objects from the black base] Mostly to take them off the shelf and throw them but. Adam is sitting on the floor] Whey' do you do in the bath. Title: How to Face Life's Problems Life is full of challenges and obstacles that can often seem insurmountable. However, with the right mindset and tools, you can learn to face life's problems head-on and come out stronger on the other side. This guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation of how to approach and overcome the difficulties that arise in your daily life. **Step 1: Identify the problem** The first step in facing any problem is recognizing and acknowledging its existence. It might be tempting to ignore or minimize issues, but doing so only delays finding a solution. To identify the problem, take some time to reflect on what is causing distress or discomfort in your life. Once you have pinpointed the source of the issue, move on to the next step. Key tip: Be honest with yourself during this process; admitting there's a problem is crucial for addressing it effectively. **Step 2: Gather information** After identifying the problem, gather as much relevant information as possible about the situation. Understanding all aspects of the challenge allows you to make informed decisions when seeking solutions. Research online, talk to experts, consult friends, family members, or colleagues who may have faced similar situations, and consider various perspectives before moving forward. Guideline: Make sure the sources of information are reliable and trustworthy. Misinformation can lead to poor decision-making and hinder progress towards resolving the problem. **Step 3: Analyze potential solutions** With a clear understanding of the problem and available information, brainstorm potential solutions. Think creatively and critically about each option, considering their pros and cons. Narrow down the list to the most feasible options based on resources, time constraints, and personal preferences. Key tip: Remember that sometimes unconventional approaches can yield successful results - don't limit yourself to traditional methods if they aren't suitable for your specific circumstances. **Step 4: Develop an action plan** Once you have identified one or more promising solutions, create a detailed action plan. Break down each solution into smaller tasks with deadlines, assign responsibilities (if applicable), and allocate necessary resources. Having a well-structured plan provides direction and motivation while reducing feelings of overwhelm. Guideline: Prioritize tasks according to importance and urgency, focusing on high-impact activities first. This strategy ensures efficient use of time and energy. **Step 5: Implement the action plan** Put your action plan into motion by completing tasks systematically. Monitor progress regularly, adjusting strategies as needed based on feedback or changing circumstances. Maintain flexibility throughout the implementation process, as unexpected challenges may require modifications to the original plan. Key tip: Stay committed to the chosen course of action but remain open to alternative paths if initial efforts prove unsuccessful. Perseverance is essential for overcoming adversity. **Step 6: Evaluate outcomes** After implementing your action plan, assess whether the problem has been resolved satisfactorily. If not, determine which elements of the solution were effective and which ones fell short. Use this knowledge to refine your approach and try again. Repeat steps 3 through 6 until the issue is fully addressed. Guideline: Celebrate small victories along the way to maintain motivation and boost self-confidence. Facing life's problems can be challenging, so acknowledging accomplishments helps sustain momentum. By following these six steps, you'll develop the skills necessary to tackle any difficulty that comes your way. Keep practicing and honing your problem-solving abilities, and remember that every challenge presents an opportunity for growth and learning. Just like the body, a mind can be healthy. The measure of this is called mental health. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), there is not one way to measure mental health in all people, because there are many things in our environment that might make what is mentally healthy different from one person to another. In general, most experts agree that "mental health" and "mental illness" are not opposites. In other words, not having a mental illness does not mean you are in good mental health. It is the east, and Juliet is the sun. Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon, Who is already sick and pale with grief, That thou her maid art far more fair than she: Be not her maid, since she is envious; Her vestal livery is but sick and green And none but fools do wear it; cast it off. It is my lady, O, it is my love! O, that she knew she were! She speaks yet she says nothing: what of that? Her eye discourses; I will answer it. I am too bold, 'tis not to me she speaks: Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven, Having some business, do entreat her eyes To twinkle in their spheres till they return. What if her eyes were there, they in her head? The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars, As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven Would through the airy region stream so bright That birds would sing and think it were not night. See, how she leans her cheek upon her hand! O, that I were a glove upon that hand, That I might touch that cheek! "What Light Through Yonder Window Breaks?" Title: How to Check Your Hard Drive Checking your hard drive is an essential task that ensures the longevity and optimal performance of your computer. By regularly checking your hard drive, you can identify potential issues early, prevent data loss, and maintain system stability. This guide will provide you with in-depth instructions on how to check your hard drive using various tools and methods available on Windows and macOS platforms. **Table of Contents:** 1. [Preparation](#prep) 2. [Windows Users: Using Chkdsk (Check Disk)](#chkdsk) 3. [macOS Users: Using Disk Utility](#diskutil) 4. [Additional Tools for Comprehensive Analysis](#addtools) 5. [Scheduling Regular Checks](#schedule) 6. [Interpreting Results and Taking Action](#interpret) 7. [Key Tips and Guidelines](#tips) 8. [Conclusion](#conclusion) <a name="prep"></a> ## 1. Preparation Before starting, ensure you have administrative access to your computer as these procedures require elevated permissions. Also, remember to save all open files and close any applications before proceeding to avoid possible data corruption or loss. Lastly, make sure you have enough free space on your hard drive; some utilities might need temporary storage during the scan process. <a name="chkdsk"></a> ## 2. Windows Users: Using Chkdsk (Check Disk) Chkdsk is a built-in command-line tool in Windows designed to detect and repair file system errors, bad sectors, and inconsistencies within a specified volume. Here's how to use it: 1. **Open Command Prompt as Administrator**: Press `Win + X` keys simultaneously and select 'Command Prompt (Admin)' from the list. If you don't see this option, try searching for 'cmd' in the Start Menu, right-click on 'Command Prompt', and choose 'Run as administrator'. Click 'Yes' when prompted by User Account Control. ![Open Command Prompt as Admin](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/f/fc/Check-Your-Hard-Drive-Step-1.jpg/aid1094435-v4- Right on her face 'cause I want to brush her hair. Title: How to Be a Baby Maid on Club Penguin Club Penguin was a popular online multiplayer game that allowed players to create their own penguin character and explore a virtual world filled with fun activities and social interactions. One unique role that some players adopted was that of a baby maid, which involved dressing up as a maid while acting and role-playing as if they were a baby. While Club Penguin is no longer available, this guide can still serve as a helpful resource for those interested in similar role-play scenarios in other online games or communities. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to be a baby maid on Club Penguin: ### Step 1: Create Your Penguin Character To start, you need to create your penguin character on Club Penguin. Choose a name, select your penguin's appearance (skin color, shape, etc.), and customize its initial clothing options. Keep in mind that there are no specific requirements for what your penguin should look like when aiming to become a baby maid; however, selecting a more youthful appearance might help reinforce the infantile aspect of the baby maid role. ### Step 2: Acquire Appropriate Clothing Items Once you have created your penguin character, browse through the catalogues to find suitable clothing items that resemble a traditional French maid outfit. Key components include: * A black dress or skirt with white apron * Black knee-high socks or stockings * White gloves * A headpiece or hat (optional) Keep in mind that actual baby clothes aren't typically part of the Club Penguin catalogue, so focus on finding pieces that fit the overall theme of being both a baby and a maid at once. Additionally, since you cannot purchase all necessary items simultaneously due to inventory restrictions, patience will be required as you gradually collect these items over time. ### Step 3: Role-Play As A Baby Embrace an immature persona by typing in broken sentences, using simplified vocabulary, and incorporating childlike behaviors such as crying, throwing tantrums, or needing assistance with simple tasks. This behavior may seem unusual within most gaming contexts but remember, consistency is crucial when committing to a role-play scenario. ### Step 4: Interact With Others Seek out like-minded individuals who share an interest in baby maid role-play. Engage them in conversations, participate in group chats related to the topic, and join dedicated servers where such activities take place. By doing so, you can build connections, learn from others, and enhance your overall experience. ### Step 5: Adhere To Community Guidelines Always respect Club Penguin's Terms of Service and community rules. Refrain from engaging in any form of cyberbullying, harassment, or explicit content. Remember that although you are adopting a unique role, maintaining a safe and positive environment for everyone remains paramount. Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Consistency is vital - Fully commit to your chosen role and maintain it throughout your gameplay. 2. Patience is essential - Collecting the right clothing items takes time due to inventory limitations. 3. Respect boundaries - Not every player enjoys role-play scenarios; always ask before joining someone else's game. 4. Stay age-appropriate - Even though you're playing as a 'baby', keep language and actions suitable for children. 5. Collaboration over competition - Focus on building relationships and sharing experiences rather than winning or losing. that's not how crayons go in there. Title: How to Deal with an Annoying Person in Your Xbox Live Party Introduction: Playing games on Xbox Live can be a fun and enjoyable experience, but sometimes you may encounter annoying individuals who ruin the party atmosphere. This guide will provide you with various steps to handle such situations effectively while maintaining respect and positivity. Remember that everyone deserves to have a good time, so let's learn how to deal with these annoyances without causing unnecessary drama or conflict. Step 1: Identify the Issue Analyze the behavior of the person in question. Are they spamming chat, using offensive language, or being excessively loud? Understanding the issue is crucial before taking any action. By doing this, you ensure that your response addresses their specific problematic actions, making communication clearer and more constructive. Key tip: Stay calm when identifying issues; avoid jumping to conclusions based on initial impressions. Step 2: Communicate Politely If possible, communicate directly with the individual about their behavior. Use non-confrontational phrases like "Hey, I noticed something that bothered me" or "Could we try something different?" Expressing yourself politely increases the chances of them acknowledging and adjusting their conduct. Guideline: Be respectful even if they aren't reciprocating. Maintaining civility keeps conversations productive and prevents escalation. Step 3: Explain Consequences Calmly explain the impact of their behavior on others within the party. For example, excessive noise might make it difficult for people to hear game sounds or strategize. Offensive language could create discomfort among other players. Using concrete examples makes your point stronger and shows maturity. Key Tip: Avoid blaming or attacking the person; instead focus on describing objective outcomes resulting from their actions. Step 4: Suggest Solutions Offer suggestions on alternative behaviors that would improve the overall experience for all participants. For instance, suggest turning down microphone sensitivity, switching to text chat, or using less explicit language. Providing alternatives demonstrates your willingness to find common ground rather than just pointing out problems. Guideline: Make sure your suggested solutions are reasonable and considerate of the other person's perspective as well. Step 5: Set Boundaries Establish clear boundaries regarding what kind of behavior is acceptable moving forward. If someone continues to act disruptively after repeated warnings, it's essential to assert your expectations clearly. Let them know that further instances will result in stricter measures. Key Tip: Keep boundaries consistent across all members of the party to foster fairness and equality. Step 6: Seek Help When Needed If the situation becomes unmanageable or threatens the enjoyment of the entire group, don't hesitate to report the user to Xbox Live enforcement services. Their platform has mechanisms designed to maintain order and protect users from harassment or abuse. Always remember that prioritizing your comfort and safety is important. Guideline: Gather evidence (like screenshots) before reporting to strengthen your case against the offending player. Conclusion: Navigating through challenging social interactions requires patience, empathy, and effective communication skills. Dealing with annoying people in your Xbox Live party doesn't necessarily mean cutting ties immediately. Instead, following these steps allows for potential resolution and growth, enhancing the gaming experience for everyone involved. Was that the guy who gave me a ride today? Title: Understanding How Images Are Formed Using Curved Mirrors Have you ever looked into a curved mirror, like the ones found in fun houses or bathrooms? Did you notice how your reflection appears bigger, smaller, or distorted? That happens because light travels in straight lines, but when it hits a curved surface, the path changes, creating different effects. Today, we will explore how mirrors form images using basic concepts that even grade-schoolers can understand! Imagine you have a shiny, ball-shaped object, which acts like a curved mirror. The center line running through this ball is called the optical axis. Now, let's think of two points along this axis - Point A and Point B - located at distances z1 and z2 from the mirror, respectively. We want to find out where a light beam coming from Point A would intersect after hitting the mirror and being reflected towards Point B. To do so, we need to know three things: the distance from Point A to the mirror (z1), the distance from Point B to the mirror (z2), and the mirror's "focus," represented by f. Focus is simply the spot where all parallel rays meet after reflecting off the mirror. Now comes the exciting part - imagine drawing arrows representing these distances on our diagram. By adding another arrow to represent the sum of reciprocals of z1 and z2, and making sure all these arrows align with one another, something magical occurs! They create a proportionate relationship known as the Imaging Equation: 1/z1 + 1/z2 = 1/f This means that given any two values among z1, z2, and f, we can calculate the third value! Pretty cool, huh? It gets better; here's why this matters in real life. Our world has many naturally occurring curved surfaces acting as mirrors, focusing sunlight into concentrated spots, sometimes igniting fires! Indoors, designers may utilize curved mirrors strategically to brighten spaces and eliminate dark corners. And yes, those funny house mirrors make us laugh while teaching us about optics too! In summary, understanding image formation using curved mirrors involves some simple math involving distances and focus. Next time you see a curvy reflection, try figuring out its equivalent flat version based on today's lesson. Remember, science is everywhere around us waiting to be explored! The objective is to get the ball through a hoop mounted high on a backboard on the opponent's side of the court, while preventing the opponent from shooting it into your team's hoop. It is a very popular sport worldwide, played with a round and usually orange(orange-brown) ball that bounces. Basketball players mainly use skills such as dribbling, shooting, running, and jumping. Each made basket is worth two points, while a basket made from beyond the three-point line is worth three points. If a player gets into too much physical contact, they may be given free throws which are worth one point each. The game typically lasts for four-quarters and the team with the most points at the end of the four-quarters win the game. If the score is tied at the end of the game, there will be something called overtime, which is additional play time to allow one team to win the match. The eyes and brain work together with amazing efficiency. Light rays enter the front of the eye and are interpreted by the brain as images. Light rays first enter your child's eye through the cornea, the "window" of the eye. The cornea is a clear dome that helps the eyes focus. The anterior chamber is located behind the cornea and in front of the iris. The anterior chamber is filled with a fluid that maintains eye pressure, nourishes the eye, and keeps it healthy. The iris is the colored part of your child's eye. Eye color varies from person to person and includes shades of blue, green, brown, and hazel. The iris contains two sets of muscles. The muscles work to make the pupil of the eye larger or smaller. The pupil is the black circle in the center of the iris. It changes size to allow more or less light to enter your child's eye. After light comes through the pupil, it enters the lens. The lens is a clear curved disc. Muscles adjust the curve in the lens to focus clear images on the retina. The retina is located at the back of your child's eye. The inner eye, the space between the posterior chamber behind the lens and the retina, is called the vitreous body. It is filled with a clear gel substance that gives the eye its shape. Light rays pass through the gel on their way from the lens to the retina. The retina is a thin tissue layer that contains millions of nerve cells. The nerve cells are sensitive to light. Cones and rods are specialized receptor cells. Cones are specialized for color vision and detailed vision, such as for reading or identifying distant objects. Cones work best with bright light. The greatest concentration of cones is found in the macula and fovea at the center of the retina. The macula is the center of visual attention. The fovea is the site of visual acuity or best visual sharpness. Rods are located throughout the rest of the retina. The eyes contain more rods than cones. Rods work best in low light. Rods perceive blacks, whites, and grays, but not colors. They detect general shapes. Rods are used for night vision and peripheral vision. High concentrations of rods at the outer portions of the retina act as motion detectors in your child's peripheral or side vision. The receptor cells in the retina send nerve messages about what your child sees to the optic nerve. The optic nerves extend from the back of each eye and join together in the brain at the optic chiasm. From the optic chiasm, the nerve signals travel along two optic tracts, and eventually to the occipital cortex where vision is processed and perceived. I thinked I think I thought. LETTER LXIX. EVELINA IN CONTINUATION. Title: How to Restore Your Old Candy Crush Game Introduction: Candy Crush Saga is one of the most popular match-three puzzle games available on various platforms like Facebook, Android, and iOS. Many players have invested significant time and effort into advancing through levels and building up their progress in this game. However, there may be situations where you lose access to your old Candy Crush game due to deleting the app or switching devices. This tutorial will guide you through several methods to help you recover your lost Candy Crush game data. Method 1 - Logging back into Facebook (For Mobile and Web Versions): If you previously linked your Candy Crush account with your Facebook profile, you can quickly restore your game by logging back into that same Facebook account on your device or web browser. Here's how: Step 1 - Install the Candy Crush App: For mobile users, download and install the latest version of Candy Crush from Google Play Store or Apple App Store. If using a computer, navigate to the Candy Crush official website at <https://www.candycrushsaga.com/> and click 'Play Now.' Key Tip: Make sure you are logged out of all other Facebook accounts before proceeding. Step 2 - Launch the Game: Open the installed Candy Crush application on your device or visit the Candy Crush page in your web browser. Step 3 - Connect to Facebook: Tap the 'Connect' button when prompted during loading screens, usually located near the top right corner of the screen, or look for an icon resembling three horizontal lines ('hamburger menu') and select 'Settings > Connect to Facebook'. Enter your login credentials if required. Explanation: By connecting your game to your Facebook account again, Candy Crush should automatically detect any previous saved progress associated with that account and allow you to continue playing from where you left off. Method 2 - Contact King Support (All Platforms): Suppose Method 1 does not work or you did not link your Candy Crush account to Facebook. In that case, reaching out to King Customer Support might be necessary. They may assist in retrieving your lost progress based on backup files or related information provided. Follow these steps: Step 1 - Visit the King Help Center: Navigate to the King official support site at <https://care.king.com/>. Step 2 - Submit a Request: Click on 'Submit a request' at the upper right corner of the page. Fill out the form accurately, providing as much detail about your issue as possible. Be prepared to provide relevant information such as your Player ID (found within the settings section of the Candy Crush app), email address used for registration, level reached, date of last play session, etc. Key Guideline: Remember to explain your situation thoroughly and politely to increase chances of successful recovery. Step 3 - Wait for Response: Allow some time for customer service agents to review and respond to your ticket. Depending on demand, responses could take anywhere from hours to days. Once they reply, carefully follow their instructions to regain access to your old Candy Crush game. Additional Tips & Precautions: * Regularly save your game progress by linking it to either a King or Facebook account. You can do this via Settings inside the Candy Crush app. * Avoid uninstalling the game without saving your current level unless absolutely necessary. * Keep track of your Player ID and password securely. * Don't share sensitive information publicly regarding your Candy Crush account. Stick to communicating directly with King Customer Support for privacy reasons. When you're going to bed he's waking up. Here comes the cat and the policeman and the mommy. That [putting silver man across the top of two silver sticks] was a good idea. Run Fast, Read Fast! Growing Independence and Comprehension is an important part of learning to read. Beginning readers can spend so much of their effort trying to decode the words, that they do not spend much time comprehending what they read. These readers need to learn fluency, which is the ability to recognize words accurately and automatically. Fluent readers use less of their time decoding, leaving more time to comprehension. This lesson will help students become more fluent through repeated readings of text. Students will be working with partners to help them learn different decoding strategies from their peers and to get more time to read. In order to become faster readers who read with more expression, one must develop fluency in their reading. Fluent reading is the ability to recognize words automatically, and being able to read them easily. Fluent reading is important in developing silent reading skills as well as a child's overall reading success. The design of this particular lesson is to offer repetitive readings of texts for students so that they become more fluent readers, and to help students understand how significant fluent reading is. -Speed Reading Record for each student -Partner check sheet for each student -Stop Watches - one for each pair of students -Pencils for students -Speed Reading Record: - After 1st read _______ - After 2nd read _______ - After 3rd read _______ -Partner Check Sheet for students to assess their partner's fluency: When I listened to my partner read: 1. Remembered more words _______ _______ 2. Read faster _______ _______ 3. Read smoother _______ _______ 4. Read with expression _______ _______ 1. Class, it is important that we learn to read with speed so that we can read things quickly and easily. Reading a story over and over again will help us all to become more fluent readers. I will then ask what they think fluency means, and we will discuss its definition and its importance as a class. Today we are going to read a story several times so that each of us will become more fluent readers. I think every one should set a goal to read faster each time we read a book. 2. I will remind the students about the using their cover-ups so that the students can use this strategy while reading. Remember whenever you come across difficult words while reading, you can use your cover-up to help you figure out what the word is. I will write the word bridge on the board, and model how to use the cover-up by decoding this word just to give them an example. As you come across a difficult word, use your cover-up to help you sound the word out. I will cover up all the letters except the and pronounce the sound of the short i=/i/. Then I am going to uncover the letters one at a time leading up to the vowel, b=/b/, r=/r/, and dge =/j/. Notice that these three sounds together make the /j/ sound. Then I will start at the beginning and tell them, b-b-r-r=/br/ now add the /i/ sound, b-r-i-i, now put the /j/ sound on the end. B-r-i-d-g-e! good! 3. Next I am going to model fluent reading. I am going to write the following sentence on the board: I like to lick my yummy ice-cream cone. Listen closely as I read this sentence to you. At first I am going to read it slowly without fluency. I ... like ... to ... lick ... my ...yummy ice ...cream ... cone. I like ... to lick... my yummy ice ... cream cone. I like to lick... my...yummy ice-cream cone. In order to get better at reading this sentence, I'll need to read it again and again. Doing this repetitively will help me read quicker so that I can read with more expression. Now I will show you how to read it faster with fluency: I like to lick my yummy ice-cream cone. Does this sound better to you when I talk quicker and is it easier to understand? It is much easier for everyone to listen when something is read fluently and quickly. It is boring if a teacher stands up and reads slow and messes up her words. That is why we need to practice reading fluently and quickly. When we read to each other, we want it to be easy to listen to so that we can focus on the meaning of the words. 4. Now I will pass out the book Kite Day at Pine Lake to each student. Has anyone ever flown a kite before? Yes, me too! This book is a story about a bunch of children that love to fly kites. They have kites of all shapes, sizes, and colors. Bob is upset because he does not have a kite. I wonder what will happen. Do you think the kids will make Bob a kite? What do you think will happen? Let's read and find out! Students will read the book on their own. Afterwards, we will discuss the story as a class and ask questions to see their comprehension and understanding of the text. They will each have a chance to read it by themselves before reading it aloud with a partner for timed readings. 5. Next, I am going to split the students up into groups of 2. I will explain to the students about the Speed Reading Record Sheet and Fluency Literacy Rubric. When you break into pairs, one of you will be the reader and the other will be the recorder. The reader will read the book for one minute three different times. The recorder will start and stop the stop watch and will announce when it is the time to begin and end after the end of one minute. The recorder will also write down the number of words read in one minute on the record sheet. After the reader has read three times, you will swap it up and do the same thing again. 6. When students have finished recording the one minute read alouds, I will have them fill out a Fluency Literacy Sheet about their partner. They will check the boxes on how the student performed on the 2nd and 3rd times reading the book for one minute. The students will each bring me their Speed Reading Record and their Partner Checklist. Each student will do one minute reads individually for me so that I can check for fluency and accuracy. I will also evaluate the reading record and partner checklist they turned in. Cushman, Sheila and Rona Kornblum. Kite Day Eldredge, J. Lloyd. (1995) Teaching Decodable in Holistic Classrooms.
