Title: How to Deal With the Death of a Best Friend Introduction: Losing a best friend is one of the most difficult experiences anyone can go through. Grief can feel overwhelming, and it's normal to experience a range of emotions such as shock, denial, anger, guilt, and profound sadness. This guide will provide you with steps and advice on how to cope during this challenging time. Remember that everyone's grieving process is unique, so take your time and be gentle with yourself. Step 1: Allow Yourself to Feel the Pain * Give yourself permission to mourn and accept the reality of the loss. * Understand that experiencing grief is a natural response to losing someone close. * Suppressing or avoiding feelings of pain may hinder the healing process. Key tip: Recognize that there is no specific timeline for dealing with grief; allow yourself to feel the emotions as they come. Step 2: Seek Support from Loved Ones * Reach out to family members, mutual friends, or other supportive individuals who understand your loss. * Talk about your feelings and memories of your best friend. * Accept help when offered - whether it's assistance with daily tasks, emotional support, or simply having company. Guidelines: * Identify people in your life whom you trust and feel comfortable talking to about your feelings. * Be open to receiving help even if you prefer to handle things independently. Step 3: Take Care of Your Physical Health * Maintain a healthy diet, exercise regularly, and get enough sleep. * Avoid turning to unhealthy coping mechanisms like excessive drinking, drug use, or overeating. Key Tips: * Engaging in physical activity releases endorphins which can improve mood and energy levels. * A balanced diet supports overall well-being and resilience during stressful times. Step 4: Create a Routine * Establish a daily routine to bring structure and stability into your life following the loss. * Gradually reintroduce activities that were paused due to grief. Guidelines: * Start by incorporating small tasks into your day before moving onto more demanding activities. * Don't rush the process; give yourself time to adjust to new routines. Step 5: Memorialize Your Best Friend * Honor your best friend's memory by creating a memorial space, writing a letter, or participating in an activity that celebrates their life. * Share stories and memories with others who knew them. Key Tip: Memorializing your friend can help you find comfort and closure while keeping their spirit alive. Step 6: Seek Professional Help If Needed * Consult a mental health professional if your grief becomes debilitating or persists for an extended period. * Consider joining a support group where you can connect with others going through similar experiences. Guidelines: * Look for therapists specializing in grief counseling or bereavement services. * Attend a few different support groups to find one that feels right for you. Conclusion: Remember that dealing with the death of a best friend takes time, patience, and self-compassion. By acknowledging your feelings, seeking support, taking care of your physical health, establishing routines, memorialized your friend, and reaching out for professional help when needed, you can navigate this difficult journey and begin to heal. Title: How to Get Push Notifications for Instagram Posts and Stories on Android Instagram is a popular social media platform that allows users to share photos and videos with their followers. One convenient feature of Instagram is push notifications, which alert you when someone likes or comments on your posts, follows you, or sends you a message. However, Instagram doesn't send notifications for every post or story from accounts you follow by default. This tutorial will guide you through enabling push notifications for specific Instagram posts and stories on your Android device. ### Section 1: Enabling Push Notifications for Individual Accounts **Step 1:** Open the **Instagram app** on your Android device. If you aren't already logged into your account, enter your credentials to log in. *Logging in ensures that you have access to all your settings and can customize them as needed.* **Step 2:** Tap on the **magnifying glass icon** at the bottom of the screen to open the search page. Enter the username of the account you want to receive notifications for in the search bar, then tap on the appropriate result. *By searching for an individual user, you narrow down the list of options and ensure that you are adjusting notifications for the correct account.* ![Magnifying Glass Icon](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Enable-Push-Notifications-for-an-Individual-on-Instagram-Step-3.jpg/aid4570869-v4- D' you want to read it to me? For thousands of years, people had no need for a name for the "Solar System". They thought the Earth stayed still at the center of everything (geocentrism). Although the Greek philosopher Aristarchus of Samos suggested that there was a special order in the sky, Nicolaus Copernicus was the first to develop a mathematical system that described what we now call the "solar system". This was called a new "system of the world". In the 17th century, Galileo Galilei, Johannes Kepler and Isaac Newton began helping people understand physics more clearly. People began to accept the idea that the Earth is a planet and moves around the Sun, and that the planets are worlds with the same physical laws that control Earth. More recently, telescopes and space probes have led to discoveries of mountains and craters, and seasonal meteorological phenomena such as clouds, dust storms and ice caps on the other planets. Hide Character Preview on an iPhone: A Comprehensive Guide The character preview feature on the iOS keyboard can be quite helpful when typing messages or notes, as it allows you to see the characters you've recently typed. However, some users might find this feature distracting or unnecessary, especially if they prefer focusing solely on the text input field without any distractions. If you belong to this category and wish to hide the character preview on your iPhone, then you're at the right place! This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process in simple steps while providing in-depth explanations and key tips along the way. Let's get started! Step 1: Access Settings App To begin with, locate the 'Settings' app on your iPhone's home screen or app library. The icon is gear-shaped, making it easy to identify. Tapping on the 'Settings' app opens up its menu where various system settings are available for customization. By accessing the Settings app, we gain control over our device's functionality and appearance, including hiding the character preview on the keyboard. Step 2: Navigate to General Settings Once inside the 'Settings' app, scroll down until you reach the 'General' section - it should be among the top options within the list. Clicking on 'General' takes us further into the configuration settings related to core aspects like language & region, software updates, and keyboard preferences. It also enables us to manage specific features that aren't part of other categories, which brings us closer to finding the option to disable the character preview. Step 3: Select Keyboard Options Within the 'General' settings, look for the subheading labeled 'Keyboard.' Underneath this heading, there will be several options pertaining to the virtual keyboard used by your iPhone. Choosing 'Keyboard' here provides access to adjustments concerning autocorrect, predictive text, emoji suggestions, and-most importantly-the character preview. Step 4: Disable Character Preview Now, finally, after navigating through these menus, you'll arrive at the coveted setting: 'Character Preview.' To hide the character preview on your iPhone's keyboard, simply toggle off this switch. Once disabled, the keyboard will no longer display the small pop-up showing the last character(s) entered. Instead, it will only showcase the keys being pressed, giving you a cleaner and less cluttered typing experience. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Keep in mind that disabling the character preview may slightly increase the likelihood of typos since you won't have visual confirmation of the most recent character entered. However, many users find this tradeoff worthwhile for a more streamlined view of their text input field. * Remember that toggling off the character preview affects all applications using the default iOS keyboard. If you ever want to revert this change, just navigate back to the same location (Settings > General > Keyboard) and enable the 'Character Preview' switch again. * Some third-party keyboards may offer similar customization options. If you're utilizing one such keyboard, consult its respective settings within the app or via the installed apps list under 'Settings' to modify the character preview behavior accordingly. In conclusion, hiding the character preview on an iPhone involves delving into the device's general settings, specifically targeting the keyboard configurations. With a few taps, you can now enjoy a minimalistic typing environment free from intrusive character previews. We hope this extensive tutorial has been beneficial in helping you personalize your iPhone experience according to your preference! Physicists widely doubt that quantum particles are truly 0D points as represented in Standard Model, which offers "formalism"-mathematical devices whose strokes predict phenomena of interest upon input of data-not "interpretation" of the mechanisms determining those phenomena. Yet string theorists do tend to optimistically conjecture that the strings are both real and explanatory, not merely predictive devices. It is far beyond the capacity of today's particle accelerators to propel any probing particles at energy levels high enough to overcome a quantum particle's own energy and determine whether it is a string. Yet this limitation also exists on testing other theories of quantum gravity. Developments suggest other strategies to "observe" the structure of quantum particles. Hello young mathematicians! Today, we are going to learn about continuity, which is a big word that means "smoothness." Imagine you are drawing a picture with a crayon. When you move your crayon smoothly without stopping or tearing the paper, you get a nice and continuous line. That's what continuity looks like in math! Now, let's think about a special kind of function, called a mapping. A mapping takes points from one group (which we call a domain) and changes them into new points in another group (the range). Let's say our domain is a bunch of number pairs, like (1,2), (3,4), and so on. Our mapping will change these pairs into something different, maybe (2,1), (4,3), etc. Our challenge today is to show that a specific mapping is smooth, meaning it doesn't stop or jump when moving through the groups. This particular mapping sends each point $(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots,x_{n+1})$ in a space called "$E^{n+1}$ minus zero" to a new point $\left(\frac{x_{1}}{\lvert x \rvert^{2}},\ldots,\frac{x_{n+1}}{\lvert x \rvert^{2}}\right)$. It may seem scary, but don't worry-we will make it easy peasy! To understand continuity for our mapping, imagine having a tiny rubber band around each pair of numbers. We want to stretch or shrink those rubber bands equally in every direction. In doing so, our original points become the new ones, forming a perfect, unbroken path between the two. As long as we can do this stretching without ripping anything apart, our mapping stays continuous! So grab some imaginary rubber bands, practice stretching them evenly, and soon you'll see why this fancy mapping is indeed continuous. Happy experimenting! Positive symptoms are thoughts, behaviors, or anything experienced by the senses that are not shared by others - like hearing voices that are not really there. They are called 'positive' not because they are good but because they are "added on". These symptoms may include having strange thoughts that do not make sense (delusions), disorganized thoughts and speech, and feeling, hearing, seeing, smelling, or tasting things that do not exist (hallucinations). Positive symptoms often respond to drug treatment and cognitive behavioral therapy, CBT. Stopping anti-psychotic or anti-vomiting drugs too quickly may also cause these symptoms. Looks like they wounded it up and this little bird's flying. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Mawhinney/000827b.cha = = = Title: How to Deal with a Bad Kisser Introduction: Kissing is an intimate act that can bring two people closer together. However, if one partner is a bad kisser, it can be uncomfortable and even off-putting. This article will provide you with steps and advice on how to deal with a bad kisser while maintaining a positive relationship. Remember, communication is key when addressing any issues within a romantic relationship. Always approach this topic with kindness, understanding, and patience. Step 1: Identify the Issues Before confronting your partner about their kissing technique, take some time to identify what makes them a bad kisser. Some common problems include: * Too much or too little saliva * Biting or using excessive force * Not moving lips enough or being too aggressive * Poor breath control * Lack of rhythm * Misaligned heads during the kiss By identifying these specific issues, you'll have a clearer idea of what needs improvement and can communicate more effectively with your partner. Step 2: Self-Reflect Consider whether there are factors contributing to your perception of their poor kissing skills. For example, maybe you had different expectations based on previous experiences or media portrayals. Or perhaps you feel nervous or self-conscious during makeout sessions, which affects your enjoyment. Reflecting on your own feelings can help ensure a constructive conversation with your partner. Step 3: Choose the Right Time and Place Find a quiet, comfortable setting where you both feel relaxed and undistracted. Make sure there's ample time for discussion without feeling rushed. Starting the conversation after a romantic moment might create unnecessary tension; instead, try talking about it at another point during the day. Step 4: Use "I" Statements When discussing the issue, focus on your personal experience rather than criticizing your partner directly. Begin sentences with "I feel..." or "In my opinion..." to avoid sounding accusatory. This approach encourages open dialogue and reduces defensiveness. For example, say something like, "I really enjoy our moments together, but I sometimes struggle with the amount of saliva during our kisses," instead of saying, "You're such a sloppy kisser." Step 5: Offer Suggestions After explaining the issues, suggest ways they could improve their kissing technique. Be specific and offer guidance tailored to the identified problem(s). You may also want to demonstrate gently by slightly adjusting your position or movements as needed. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Focus on positivity: Emphasize what you appreciate about your relationship and express gratitude for their willingness to listen and grow. * Avoid blaming language: Instead of saying "you always...", rephrase it to "sometimes I notice..." * Practice active listening: Allow your partner to share their thoughts and feelings without interruption. Show empathy and validate their emotions. * Keep it lighthearted: Although this conversation is important, try to maintain a casual tone so neither party feels overly stressed or anxious. * Follow up: After discussing the matter, continue practicing and offering gentle feedback when appropriate. Celebrate improvements along the way! Conclusion: Addressing a partner's poor kissing technique requires tact, diplomacy, and compassion. By following these steps and key tips, you can navigate this delicate situation while strengthening your bond. Ultimately, open communication fosters mutual respect and understanding, leading to deeper intimacy between partners. To put on your back if you have a safety pin. [takes a sponge with plastic handle from top of refrigerator] Make a Sight Word Book Sight words are the work horses of the English language. They're words that appear frequently in print, but words that are either difficult to sound out, or don't follow the rules. New readers need to develop a "bank" of these sight words, so they can read them quickly, without having to pause to figure them out. Want to give your kid a leg up with sight words? Put the flash cards away and pull out the construction paper! By creating his own book, your kindergartener can make something he'll be proud of, and get ten key sight words under his belt at the same time. Q: How can we detect black holes? A: There are two basic ways we can detect whether there's a black hole. Black holes are, of course, dark by definition - not even light escapes them! The first way we detect black holes is by their gravitational influence. For example, at the center of the Milky Way , we see an empty spot where all of the stars are circling around as if they were orbiting a really dense mass. That's where the black hole is. The second way is by observing the matter falling into the black hole. As matter falls in, it settles in a disk around the black hole that can get very hot. Some of the energy liberated from falling in is turned into light, which we can then see, for example, in X-rays. Please note this is a moderated blog. Today, we are going to learn about a really cool concept called "functions" and how we can optimize them using a programming tool called Scipy. Don't worry, it's not as complicated as it sounds! Firstly, let's talk about functions. A function is like a recipe or a set of instructions that takes some inputs and gives us an output based on those inputs. For example, imagine you have a lemonade stand and your recipe for making lemonade requires two ingredients: water and lemons. The function would take these two inputs (water and lemons) and give you an output (lemonade). Now, let's move on to optimization. Imagine you want to make the best possible lemonade with the least amount of resources. You could try different combinations of water and lemons until you find the perfect balance that makes the tastiest lemonade while using the fewest resources. This process of finding the optimal solution is what optimization is all about. In the world of coding, we often encounter problems where we need to optimize a certain function to get the desired outcome. And that's where Scipy comes in! Scipy is a powerful Python library that provides various tools for scientific computing, including optimization algorithms. One such algorithm is the `scipy.optimize.rosen` function, which helps us optimize a specific type of function known as the Rosenbrock function. The Rosenbrock function is a mathematical equation that looks like this: f(x) = sum(100\*(x\_ [mother points to a puzzle with geometric shapes.] An after you do that ya wanna want to read a story? Lumis This strong mainstay of the tree begins as a tender stem from which leaves begin to sprout. The trunk is the body of the tree, which not only supports the crown, but in addition internally channels sap and tree food from one part of the tree to another. A tree grows taller by adding new growth at the tip. In the spring, a new shoot starts to grow at the very tip of the tree. This is called the leader. Its length indicates how much a tree has grow over the course of a year. New shoots grow out sideways from the base of the leader. Each end of each branch has a similar growth of shoots. By summers' end, buds form on the new shoots, and from these buds will develop next year's shoots. The girth of a tree develops in quite a different way. Between the bark and the wood is a thin soft layer called the cambium. Each year this cambium produces a new layer of wood. You cannot see these layers as they are hidden by the bark. However, the age of cut trees may be determined by counting these layers- called annual growth rings - on the stump. How you erase one more thing like erase this. Examples of legislatures include the Houses of Parliament in London, the Congress in Washington, D.C., the Bundestag in Berlin, the Duma in Moscow and the Assembl e nationale in Paris. Most legislatures have two chambers or houses, a 'lower house' and an 'upper house'. To pass legislation, a majority of Members of Parliament must vote for a bill in each house. The legislature is the branch of government that writes laws, and votes on whether they will be approved. What are you going to do with them? Uh yeah do you have favorite foods? She said I could do more chores to earn more money. I worked and worked! Mom gave me some money! I went right upstairs and put it in my piggy bank! I really learned a lesson about money! Title: How to Braid Your Hair Like Anna's From Frozen Introduction: Anna from Disney's Frozen has a unique hairstyle that combines two French braids with a loose fishtail braid at the back. This intricate yet charming look is perfect for special occasions or cosplay events. With patience and practice, you can recreate this style at home by following these steps. **Materials Needed:** - A brush or comb - Four hair ties (preferably clear) - Bobby pins (optional) - Hairspray (for hold; optional) Step 1: Brush Your Hair Before starting any braid, make sure your hair is smooth and free of tangles. Use a detangling brush or wide-tooth comb to remove all knots gently. Starting with tangle-free hair makes it easier to create neat and even sections as you braid. Step 2: Section Off the Top Layer Take about one inch of hair near your natural parting line on both sides of your head. Clip away the rest of your hair using clips or hairpins so it doesn't get mixed up while working on the initial braids. Step 3: Create Two Dutch Braids Dutch braiding is similar to French braiding but involves crossing pieces under instead of over. Beginning at the crown of your head, start creating two dutch braids - one for each section of hair you separated earlier. Here are the steps for a single dutch braid which should be repeated twice: 1. Divide the section into three equal parts. 2. Cross the right strand under the middle strand. The police even helped clean up the mess! Jimmy and his family thanked the police before they left and Jimmy finally got to continue playing with his toys. [Nina puts hat on her head and mother puts a hat on her own head] Hide Recent Changes Widget in wikiHow The "Recent Changes" widget on wikiHow can be a useful tool for keeping track of updates and edits made by users across the website. However, there may be instances where you'd like to hide this widget from your view while browsing or editing articles. This tutorial will guide you through the process of hiding the recent changes widget on wikiHow using custom JavaScript code. **Step 1: Log into Your Account** First, log into your wikiHow account. If you don't have an account yet, create one as you need to be logged in to edit your user preferences. Having an account allows you to personalize your experience on the platform and engage with the community more effectively. **Step 2: Access User Preferences** Once logged in, click on your username located at the top right corner of any page. From the dropdown menu, select "User Profile." On your profile page, find the "Preferences" tab on the left sidebar and click on it. This action takes you to your user preference settings. **Step 3: Locate Gadgets Section** On the Preferences page, scroll down until you see the "Gadgets" section. This is where you can enable or disable various features that enhance your wikiHow experience. By default, many gadgets are already enabled; however, we will add some custom JavaScript code to achieve our goal. **Step 4: Add Custom JavaScript Code** In the "Custom JavaScript" text box under the Gadgets section, paste the following code snippet: ```javascript $(document).ready(function() { $('.recentchanges-widget').hide(); }); ``` This jQuery script listens for when the document is fully loaded (using `$(document).ready`) before executing its function, which hides the element with class 'recentchanges-widget.' Since the recent changes widget has this specific class name, the script successfully targets it and removes it from display. **Step 5: Save Preferences** After pasting the code, ensure all other settings are configured according to your preferences. Once satisfied, click the "Save" button at the bottom of the page. The system then saves your new configuration, including the hidden recent changes widget. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Always double-check that you have copied the correct code snippet without errors or typos. A single mistake could cause unintended issues or prevent the code from functioning correctly. * Be cautious about sharing your custom JavaScript code publicly since malicious actors might exploit it if they gain access to your account. Only share your code privately or within trusted circles. * Remember that disabling the recent changes widget does not affect others viewing the same pages. It only modifies what you personally see during your session. Other users will still have the widget displayed unless they take similar steps to remove it themselves. * Keep your browser up-to-date to avoid compatibility issues with JavaScript codes and improve overall security. Internet Explorer in 2008 had a number of published security vulnerabilities. According to research done by security research firm Secunia, Microsoft did not respond as quickly as its competitors in fixing security holes and making patches available. The firm also reported 366 vulnerabilities in ActiveX controls, an increase from the prior year. [Karen and Linda both live near Nina.] Hey look there's more to the bee. And she wanted to go say hi. [all the pieces of soldier have been taken off. Nina is starting to put the soldier back together again.] Sometimes, even the torque of a diesel engine is not enough to run such big machines. To boost power, large diesels often have a device called a turbocharger attached. A turbocharger is a type of turbine - something used to move a lot of air very quickly. Jet engines also contain a turbine. In the diesel, pressure from the exhaust spins the turbocharger at a very high speed. Fresh air is then forced back into the engine. Since the engine works by pumping air, the more air you can put through it, the more power it makes. That is where the turbocharger helps. A diesel engine that has a turbocharger is called a turbodiesel. The whistling sound sometimes heard near one of these engines is caused by the turbocharger, or "turbo" for short. oh, no my phone is ringing again! This is called dysmenorrhea, or cramps. Extreme and continuous cramps are not normal and should be checked upon with a medical practitionner. The hormones that are produced before and during a period can also make a woman feel moody, or just strange. This is called premenstrual syndrome (PMS), or premenstrual tension (PMT). A person can feel bloated or swollen and have long cramps. Some menstruators have extremely bad reactions to menstruation and may even feel suicidal. This is called premenstrual dysphoric disorder, or PMDD. Other physical effects include headaches, tender berasts, lower back pain, fatigue, acne, diarrrhea or constipation, trouble sleeping and more. But they look a little like this. Hand, foot and mouth disease Hand, foot and mouth disease is caused by a virus. The germs affect your child's mouth, hands and feet at the same time. The virus takes about three to five days to develop. Your child can get this disease more than once. Symptoms of hand, foot and mouth disease are: - small white spots or canker sores on the mouth or tongue - rash (blisters or bumps) on the palms of the hands and spaces between the fingers and the soles of her feet and spaces between the toes Your child can spread hand, foot and mouth disease from saliva. How to make your child more comfortable - Give your child extra liquids. - Give your child soft foods like ice cream, pudding and yogurt. Antibiotics will not help treat hand, foot and mouth disease. The virus will have to run its course. When to call your health care provider Call your health care provider for an appointment if your child refuses to drink enough to maintain a normal rate of urination. Dealing with bad roommates can be a challenging and stressful experience, but there are steps you can take to improve the situation or resolve any issues that arise. Here's a detailed guide on how to deal with bad roommates: **Step 1: Identify the Problem** The first step in dealing with a bad roommate is to identify the specific problems you are experiencing. Is your roommate messy and leaving dirty dishes around? Are they constantly bringing over guests at all hours of the night? Or are they disrespecting your personal boundaries and possessions? Clearly identifying the issue will help you determine the best course of action. Key tip: Keep a journal or log of specific incidents and examples of the problem behavior to refer back to when discussing the issue with your roommate. **Step 2: Communicate Openly and Respectfully** Once you have identified the problem, it's important to communicate openly and respectfully with your roommate about your concerns. Use "I" statements instead of blaming language (e.g., "I feel uncomfortable when " rather than "You always...") and try to avoid getting defensive or emotional. Guideline: Schedule a time to talk with your roommate in a quiet, private setting where you both can focus on the conversation. Bring up the specific issues you have noticed and explain how they are affecting you. Be sure to listen actively to their perspective as well. **Step 3: Set Clear Expectations and Boundaries** After discussing the issues with your roommate, work together to set clear expectations and boundaries for shared living spaces and responsibilities. This might include creating a chore chart, establishing rules for guests, or designating certain areas of the home as off-limits. Key tip: Make sure these expectations and boundaries are agreed upon by both parties and put them in writing, such as an email or group message, so everyone has a record of what was discussed. **Step 4: Follow Through on Agreements** It's essential to hold yourself and your roommate accountable for following through on the agreements you made in Step 3. If one person isn't pulling their weight, bring it up gently and remind them of the agreed-upon terms. Guideline: Regular check-ins or evaluations can help ensure that everyone is staying on track and addressing any potential issues before they become major problems. **Step 5: Seek Mediation if Necessary** If open communication and setting boundaries haven't resolved the issues between you and your roommate, consider seeking mediation from a neutral third party, such as a resident assistant or housing coordinator. They may be able to provide guidance, facilitate a discussion, or offer alternative solutions. Key tip: Before involving a mediator, make sure you have exhausted all other options and clearly communicated your concerns directly with your roommate. **Step 6: Consider Alternative Living Arrangements** In extreme cases where conflicts cannot be resolved, it may be necessary to explore alternative living arrangements. This could mean finding a new place to live, switching rooms, or even breaking your lease early. Guidelines: Consult your housing agreement or landlord to understand your options and possible consequences before making any decisions. Additionally, consider reaching out to campus resources, such as counseling services, for support during this process. Remember that every roommate situation is unique, so some steps may be more relevant than others depending on the circumstances. By approaching the situation calmly, respectfully, and proactively, you can increase the chances of resolving conflicts and maintaining a positive living environment. They like to play in the farm. One day, they see a new animal in the farm. It is a mule. A mule is like a horse, but with long ears and a different tail. "Hello, mule!" Tom says. "What is your name?" The mule does not answer. He looks at Tom and Sue with big eyes. He is shy. "Can we pet you, mule?" Sue asks. She walks closer to the mule. She wants to touch his soft fur. The mule does not like that. He kicks his legs and makes a loud noise. He runs away from Sue. He is scared. "Ouch!" Sue says. She falls on the ground. She hurts her knee. She cries. Tom runs to Sue. He helps her get up. He hugs her. He is sorry. "Don't cry, Sue," Tom says. "The mule did not mean to hurt you. He is new here. He does not know us. He needs time to be our friend." Sue stops crying. She nods. She understands. "Maybe we can give him some food," Tom says. "Maybe he likes apples or carrots. Then he will see we are nice." Sue smiles. She agrees. They go to the barn. They find some apples and carrots. They take them to the mule. They put them on the ground near the mule. They wait. The mule sees the food. He smells it. He is hungry. He walks to the food. He eats it. He likes it. Tom and Sue watch the mule. They are happy. They see the mule's ears move. They see his tail wag. They see his eyes glow. He is happy too. "Look, Sue," Tom says. "The mule is glowing. He likes us. He is not shy anymore." "Look, Tom," Sue says. "The mule is coming to us. He wants to be petted. He is not scared anymore." The mule comes to Tom and Sue. He lets them pet him. He makes a soft noise. He is friendly. Tom and Sue pet the mule. They talk to him. They give him a name. They call him Moe. Moe is a good mule. He likes Tom and Sue. What patterns do you see in the arrangement of the numbers? Describe each pattern using words and symbols. Exploring Pascal's Triangle As you look for patterns, try to answer the following questions: - Can you predict the next row of numbers? - Add the numbers in each row. Is there a pattern in the sums of these numbers? - Do any numbers repeat? - Can you find a pattern in the diagonal numbers? Share your discoveries with your class. See if you can find: 1, 2, 3, 4, ... 1, 3, 6, 10, ... powers of 2 2, 4, 8, 16, ... 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ... Canceling your State Farm insurance policy is a straightforward process that can be completed by following these steps: Step 1: Review Your Current Policy Before canceling your insurance, take some time to review your current policy and understand its terms and conditions. This will help you determine if there are any cancellation fees or penalties associated with ending your coverage early. Additionally, make sure you have enough alternative insurance coverage in place before canceling your State Farm policy to avoid any gaps in protection. Key Tip: Keep a copy of your current policy and any relevant documents for future reference. Step 2: Determine Why You Want to Cancel Identifying the reason why you want to cancel your State Farm insurance policy can help you decide whether cancelation is the best option. For example, if you're looking to save money, consider shopping around for cheaper rates from other insurers instead of canceling immediately. If you've experienced poor customer service or dissatisfaction with your coverage, discussing your concerns with a representative may lead to a resolution without needing to cancel. Guideline: Be aware that certain life events like moving out of state or getting married might qualify you for special discounts or new policies better suited to your needs, so explore all options before deciding to cancel. Step 3: Contact State Farm Customer Service To initiate the cancellation process, reach out to State Farm's customer service department via phone, email, or mail. The contact information should be readily available on their official website or through your local agent. When speaking with a representative, provide them with your full name, address, policy number, and the specific date you wish to terminate your coverage. Key Tip: Schedule your cancellation effective date according to your needs; for instance, ensure that it aligns with the start date of a new policy from another provider. Step 4: Confirm Receipt of Cancellation Request After communicating your intention to cancel, request written confirmation from State Farm acknowledging receipt of your cancellation request. This documentation serves as proof that you initiated the cancellation process and helps prevent any potential misunderstandings about your policy status. Guideline: Follow up with State Farm within a week after submitting your cancellation request if you haven't received written confirmation yet. Step 5: Understand Any Potential Fees or Penalties Depending on the type of insurance and the length of time remaining on your policy, you may be subject to cancellation fees or penalties. Ask your State Farm representative to clearly explain any charges associated with ending your coverage early. Key Tip: If applicable, factor in these costs when comparing prices from different insurers to ensure accurate comparisons. Step 6: Return Any Required Documents or Equipment If you have rental car insurance, a roadside assistance plan, or any other additional coverage requiring equipment (like GPS devices), return these items promptly upon canceling your policy. Failing to do so could result in extra charges being levied against you. Guideline: Retain tracking information such as shipping labels or receipts until you receive confirmation that State Farm has received the returned materials. Step 7: Set Up Alternative Insurance Coverage Once your State Farm policy has been successfully cancelled, secure an alternate form of insurance coverage to protect yourself and your assets. Research various providers, compare quotes, and select a policy that meets both your needs and budget constraints. Key Tip: Consider setting reminders for upcoming renewal dates to periodically evaluate your insurance coverage and seek competitive offers from other companies. By carefully following these steps, you can effectively cancel your State Farm insurance policy while ensuring continued protection and avoiding unnecessary complications. By Nicola Salvatico, Consulting Teacher In this activity your child uses fruit to learn about fractions. To review fractions with your child, read a book about fractions such as Eating Fractions by Bruce Mcmillan, Ed Emberley's Picture Pie: A Circle Drawing Book by Ed Emberley, Fraction Action by Loreen Leedy, Give Me Half by Stuart J. Murphy or Hershey's Fractions by Jerry Pallotta. Then take an apple or orange and cut it in half. Have your child put it together to show a whole, then one half and two halves. Reinforce how two halves make a whole. To extend the activity, cut the halves into fourths and do the same having your child put it back together to show a half, then a whole. You can then have your child eat a fourth or a half or a whole of the fruit. You can also extend this activity beyond food by having your child cut a paper plate into halves and quarters. Title: How to Clean Tarnished Jewelry with Toothpaste Introduction: Jewelry can tarnish over time due to various factors such as exposure to air, moisture, or chemicals. While there are many commercial jewelry cleaners available, toothpaste is an effective and affordable alternative that you might already have at home. This tutorial will guide you through the process of cleaning tarnished jewelry using toothpaste. Materials Needed: 1. Non-gel toothpaste (preferably white) 2. Soft-bristle toothbrush 3. Small bowl or container 4. Warm water 5. Soft cloth or microfiber towel 6. Gloves (optional) Step 1: Prepare Your Workspace and Materials Gather all necessary materials before starting the cleaning process. Protect your workspace by covering it with newspaper or a disposable tablecloth. Put on gloves if you have sensitive skin to prevent any irritation from the toothpaste. Step 2: Create a Toothpaste Paste Squeeze a small amount of non-gel toothpaste into a bowl. Add a few drops of warm water and mix well until you create a paste-like consistency. Using too much water may make the solution less effective, while using too little may make it difficult to spread evenly on your jewelry. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Use only plain, non-gel toothpaste for this method. Gel toothpastes contain added ingredients like moisturizers and artificial colors that could damage or discolor your jewelry. - White toothpaste works best because it does not contain abrasive particles found in colored toothpastes, which can scratch delicate metals and gemstones. Step 3: Apply the Toothpaste Mixture Dip the soft-bristle toothbrush into the toothpaste paste. Gently scrub the surface of the tarnished jewelry using circular motions. Make sure to cover all areas of the piece but be careful not to force the toothpaste into crevices or settings where stones are set, as this could loosen them. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Be gentle when scrubbing; applying too much pressure can cause scratches on softer metals like gold and silver. - Avoid using this method on pearls, opals, emeralds, turquoise, and other porous or fragile gems, as they may become damaged or discolored. Step 4: Rinse Off the Toothpaste Rinely the jewelry under running warm water, making sure to remove all traces of toothpaste. If needed, gently rub the item with your fingers to help lift off any remaining residue. Step 5: Dry Thoroughly Pat the cleaned jewelry dry with a soft cloth or microfiber towel. It's important to ensure that every part of the piece is completely dried to prevent future tarnishing. Laying the jewelry out flat in a safe place allows it to air dry thoroughly. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Never leave damp jewelry lying around, as excess moisture can lead to further tarnishing. - Regularly maintaining your jewelry by cleaning it with toothpaste can extend its lifespan and keep it looking shiny and new. Conclusion: With just a few household items, you can effectively clean tarnished jewelry without resorting to expensive chemical cleaners. By following these steps carefully and treating your jewelry with care, you'll have it shining like new in no time! Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved drinking juice, especially the fancy kind her mommy would make for her. One day, she asked her mommy for some juice but her mommy was busy on the phone. Lily waited patiently for her mommy to finish her call. Finally, her mommy hung up the phone and turned to Lily. "What would you like, sweetie?" her mommy asked. "Juice please," Lily replied. Her mommy smiled and went to the kitchen to make her some fancy juice. Lily was so happy to have her juice and she drank it all up. "Thank you, mommy," she said. Her mommy replied, "You're welcome, my dear. Anytime you want some juice, just let me know." Lily smiled and hugged her mommy. She loved her mommy's fancy juice and her mommy's love even more. You can help your child understand how plants change and grow. What You Need - plastic container - potting soil - package of flower or vegetable seeds What You Do With your child, pour some potting soil into the plastic container. Show your child the package of seeds and talk about what will grow from the seeds. Help your child plant some seeds in the plastic container. Water the seeds with your child, and place the container in a place where it will get light. Remind your child to water the plant, and watch for it to grow. Talk with your child about the activity. By: Lauren Beno Just as students need to learn how to recognize each vowel sound, they also need to learn the concepts of vowel and consonant digraphs to become fluent readers. Children who are beginner readers need to understand that two letters joined together can represent one sound. This is known as a digraph. The digraph that we will be focusing on in this lesson contain the letters "s" and "h" which together make a /sh/ sound. This lesson will help students identify the /sh/ sound in words. The students will learn to recognize the /sh/ sound in spoken words through the use of meaningful representations such as the mouth moves, hand gestures, and tongue twisters. With these exercises, I hope that the students will be confident and expressive when reading and decoding words containing this sound. 1. Pencils and Primary Paper 3. Lower case letters for each student (a, b, c, e, f, h, i, l, l, o, p, r, s, t, u, w) in a zip-lock bag 4. Letter box squares for each student 5. Book (One Fish, Two Fish, Red Fish Blue, Blue Fish by Dr. Seuss) enough copies for each pair of students. 6. Marker board with markers 7. Tongue Twister copies for students. 8. Sh worksheet with pictures; however, it must include pictures that include the sh digraph and others that do not. Ex: ship, bag, fan, shell, sheep, fish, shoes, and cap for the matching game. 1. Students need to understand that every letter has its own mouth move and makes a particular sound. Explain to students that, "Today we are going to talk about a special phoneme. We already know the sounds s and h make when they are by themselves, but today we are going to find out the sound that they make when they are together. Whenever s and h are together, they make the special sound /sh/, like in shark and fish. The /sh/ sound can be tricky but with some practice you will be able to spot all kinds of words that make the /sh/ sound." 2. Have you ever heard a mother say /sh/ because a baby was sleeping? That's the mouth move we make when we say these words. Now I want you to put your finger to your lip and say sh. What do you feel? You feel air. We say /sh/ when we want someone to be quiet because they are too loud. 3. Let's begin by saying a tongue twister: "Shelly shops for fish food, shells, and ships." (This will be displayed on chart paper in the front of the class). Who can tell me what sound they heard the most? They will say /sh/. "Great! Now class I want everyone to say the tongue twister with me three times. Now let's say it again, but this time I want you to drag out the /sh/ in the words while making the hand gesture. Shhhhelly shhhhops for fishhhhhh food, shhhhhells, and shhhhhips. Awesome! 4. Now we are going to use individual letters and Elkonin boxes to spell words with the /sh/ digraph. When we spell words using our letterboxes we need to remember that only one sound can go into each box. "Who can tell me how many boxes I need for the word she?" Two that is right! "Who can tell me the two sounds?" They will say /sh/ and /E/. 5. Lets say that you want to spell the word "shop". Well it has three sounds which were /sh/, /o/, and /p/ (Model this for students on the marker board). In the first box we will put the /sh/ sound which would be the letters s and h. In the second box we will put the /o/ sound which would be the letter "o". In the third box we will put the /p/ sound which would be the letter "p". Great job class! Now I am going to say several words with the /sh/ digraph in them and I want you to try to spell them using your letters and letterboxes. 6. The students will spell words using individual letters and letterboxes. "Everyone take your bag of letters and your letter boxes and lay them flat on your desk. I want you to turn over your letters so that you can read each letter." The following are a list of words that the teacher will call out to the students: 3 phonemes: shell, fish, cash, shop, wish, bash 4 phonemes: brush, flush, trash 5 phonemes: splash Letters needed: a, b, c, e, f, h, i, l, l, o, p, r, s, t, u, w I am thinking of animal that lives in the sea. 2. I am thinking of something a genie can grant. 3. I am thinking of something you can find at the beach in the sand. 4. I am thinking of something you wear on your feet. 8. Pass out copies of the book One Fish, Two Fish, Red Fish, Blue Fish to each pair of students as well as a piece of primary paper and a pencil. "Do yall like fish? well this book i s all about fish of every differerent color, shape, age, size, and talent. It's a fun book to read and it has our "sh" sound in mahy words. For our next activity, I am going to divide you into pairs. Take turns reading a page from the book to each other. After you finish reading the book, write down all the words that have sh and make the /sh/ sound when you come across these words." 9. Each student will be a given a worksheet with various pictures on it that has the "sh" digraph and makes the /sh/ sound. . Some pictures will not have the digraph "sh" and won't have the /sh/ sound. There will be a row of pictures and a row of words , the students will then have to match the words witht he pictures , this will allow me to see if they have an understanding of the "sh" diagraph. Independence Lesson Design Independence Lesson Design Reading fluency is the ability to recognize words accurately, rapidly, and automatically. In order for children to really enjoy reading, and to better understand what they are reading, they must read fluently. Fluent readers must begin to read with automaticity so they can focus on the meaning of the text. Instead of having to spend time decoding individual words, a fluent reader can recognize and decode words instantly, thus allowing them comprehension and enjoyment of the text. In this lesson, students will learn how to improve their fluency by learning and using strategies such as cross checking. In this lesson, students will also increase their fluency skills with repeated readings as well as one-minute reads. Book: Pat's Jam by: Sheila Cushman. Carson, CA. : Educational Insights, c1990. (one copy per student and one for teacher) Stop watch (1 per student pair) A track made out for students to see their progress (Draw or trace a racetrack on poster board and cut out. Have a start and finish line. The track should contain numbers such as 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, etc., in which the students will place their car on the track according to how many words they read. Place sticky tack on the back of the cut out race cars with student's name and have students place on the track after reading to show the number of words read and their progression.) A cut out of a race car (One per student- Find or draw racecar and cut out. Write the student's name on the car.) Partner record sheet (1 per student): record sheet (1 per student): Name: ______________________ Date: ___________ Book: After each reading my partner, : 2nd reading 3rd reading 1. Read more words correctly yes/no yes/no 2. Read smoother yes/no yes/no 3. Read faster yes/no yes/no One minute read (1 per student): Name: _________________________ Date: ___________ Book: After 1st read _______ After 2nd read _______ After 3rd read _______ Begin by explaining to students the importance of reading fluently. "WE ALL WANT TO BECOME BETTER READERS. TODAY WE ARE GOING TO LEARN SOME WAYS TO BE MORE FLUENT SO THAT WE CAN READ FASTER THAN WE CAN RIGHT NOW. IF WE READ FASTER AND DON'T HAVE TO CONCENTRATE ON INDIVIDUAL WORDS, , WE CAN UNDERSTAND WHAT WE ARE READING AND THIS MAKES READING MORE FUN." Using crosschecking can be very useful when students get stuck on difficult words. Explain to students how they may use crosschecking. "I am going to read a sentence from a book and show you how crosschecking can be helpful. The bate is black and can fly. Wait! That didn't make sense, let me go back and check. Oh, it says the bat is black and can fly. I know this because bats can fly and they are black. Now I want you all to try. I'm going to read this sentence and I want you to use crosschecking to figure out what it should say. Raise your hand when you know. 'The girl is made because her brother broke her doll.' How can you tell what it should say?" Model how to become a fluent reader. "I AM GOING TO SHOW YOU THAT YOU CAN BECOME A FLUENT READER THE MORE YOU READ AND REREAD. write the sentence on the board: I went to the park to swing. "I-----WWWEEENNNT----TO-----THE----P---PAARRRK-----SSSOO. WAIT THAT DOESN'T SAY SO. LET ME TRY THAT AGAIN. I-----WWEENT-- WENT--TO---THE---PAARRRK-----TO----SSSSWWWIIINNNG. OH, SWING. OKAY, LET ME TRY THAT AGAIN. I-----WENT---TO---THE---PARK-----TO----SSSSWWWIIINNNG. NOW I'VE GOT IT, I WENT TO THE PARK TO SWING." Ask the students if they understood what you read the first few times. ask them why they think it was easier to understand you the last time versus the other times. Explain to students that the more fluent they become, the more they will understand what they are reading. Let the students practice reading fluently. "YOU ALL ARE GOING TO READ A BOOK CALLED PAT'S JAM BY: SHEILA CUSHMAN. PAT AND PAM ARE FRIENDS. THE TWO DRIVE TO THE STORE AND GET A HAM AND SOME JAM. WHEN THEY LEAVE THE STORE AND GET BACK IN THE VAN, THEY REALIZE THEY DON'T HAVE ANY GAS. WHAT DO YOU THINK THEY ARE GOING TO DO? WILL THEY HAVE TO WALK ALL THE WAY HOME? WELL, YOU HAVE TO READ THE STORY TO FIND OUT. I WANT YOU ALL TO READ THE BOOK TO YOURSELF AND THEN REREAD IT." Once all the students have had time to read the book, ask them some comprehension questions to check for students understanding of what they read. "WHO WERE THE MAIN CHARACTERS? DID THEY DRIVE A CAR? WHAT DID THEY GET AT THE STORE? WHAT WAS THE PROBLEM WHEN THEY LEFT THE STORE? HOW DO YOU THINK THEY GOT HOME?" Break students up into pairs. Each pair will be given a stopwatch and 2 partner Record sheet. The students will take turns reading as many words as fluently and accurately as possible in one minute. One student will read as the other one records, then they will switch. Tell them, "I WANT EACH OF YOU IN YOUR PAIRS TO READ THE STORY OUT LOUD. ONE OF YOU WILL START OFF AS THE TIMER AND RECORDER WHILE THE OTHER ONE TRIES TO READ AS FLUENTLY AND ACCURATELY AS POSSIBLE. THEN, YOU WILL SWITCH ROLES AND DO THE SAME THING. YOU WILL END UP READING THIS STORY THREE TIMES WITH YOUR PARTNER. MAKE SURE IF YOU ARE RECORDING AND TIMING THAT YOU ARE JUST PAYING ATTENTION TO HOW THEY ARE READING AND THE TIME. REMEMBER, WE DO NOT MAKE FUN OF EACH OTHER. I EXPECT YOU TO TAKE YOUR JOB SERIOUSLY DURING THIS ACTIVITY." For assessment, have each individual student come up to the teacher's desk to take a one-minute read. The students will reread the book they just read while the teacher is assessing the child's reading fluency. The teacher should have a stopwatch that beeps so that the students know when to start and stop without the teacher having to yell stop. The one minute read sheet will be used to mark their progress. There will also be a place at the bottom for comments to write in anything noticed during their one minute readings that they may need to work on. This could be things such as their breaks in between words and their voice pitch. The students will be moving their racecar as far as how many words they were able to read in the one minute on the track. I will explain to the students that their goal is to get all the way around the racetrack. Lauren Dooley, 3,2,1 READ! Tabetha Rape, Let's Go Reading Jam by: Sheila CA. : Educational Insights, c1990. Hey there! Today, let's learn about something called "Bayesian linear regression." You might be wondering, "What does that even mean?!" Well, don't worry-I'll break it down into smaller parts so it will be easier to understand. Imagine you're trying to predict how many points you'll score in your next basketball game based on the number of hours you practice each day. This kind of prediction is called a "regression line," which shows the relationship between two things. Now, here comes the Bayesian part. In real life, we know that our predictions won't always be perfect because lots of other factors could affect our performance, like our mood or whether we had a good night's sleep. So, instead of just guessing, we can use something called "probabilities" to represent our uncertainty. That way, when we make predictions, we also get a sense of how sure (or unsure) we are about them. Cool, huh?! To do all this fancy stuff, we need a computer program that helps us crunch numbers and draw these regression lines while considering uncertainties. One such tool is called "PyMC3", which stands for Python Probability Modeling Library version 3. It lets us play around with different scenarios by changing the assumptions, like how much we think practicing affects our performance. In the end, we can create cool plots showing multiple possible regression lines that consider our uncertainty. By looking at those lines together, we get a better idea of how practising relates to scoring points in basketball games. How awesome is that?! So, remember: Bayesian linear regression means making educated guesses and understanding their uncertainty to help us visualize relationships between things. And tools like PyMC3 make it fun and easy to explore these ideas! How come on the phone book it says Worcester Massachusetts east? Where's the other orange one [magnet ball]? I'm not there that much now XXX. [points to a cup to show Cha] That's for the baby that doesn't go on the hands. The San Andreas fault was discovered by Andrew Lawson in 1895, who climbed into the faulted serpentinite well where the south tower of the Golden Gate Bridge was being poured. In spite of the extreme deformation of the serpentinite, Lawson declared the bridge perfectly safe. The events of the 1906 San Francisco earthquake would seem to support his conclusion: the ruptured strand was not the one he observed in the footprint of the bridge. Soil is different in different places on Earth. This is because the climate and rocks in the Earth are different in different places on Earth. Soils are usually thicker in places where ice sheets covered the ground during the Pleistocene ice ages. This is because the ice sheets ground the rock into powder as they slowly moved over the surface. If you could pick up all your toys like this when it was on the ground I'd be set. But they still needed to buy some things and sell others. So they used shells, salt, camels, fish, beads, feathers, and lots of other things as money. That didn't work very well. Food spoiled. Salt blew away. Fish smelled! Then there was the question of value. Take a camel for example. A camel is worth lots more to someone who wants to cross the desert than to a person who wants to travel around a lake. And a young strong camel is worth more than an old tired camel. But how much more? What kind of money lasts? Not fish, that's for sure! People decided that metal would be good to use as money because it was tough, didn't spoil, and was easy to carry. And because everyone wanted precious metals like gold and silver, coins made of them had built-in value. Making coins is called "minting." Today, the United States government makes our coins at mints located in Denver and Philadelphia. They make 13 billion coins every year! How many is that? The two US mints made enough pennies last year to circle the world four times! See the "D" under the date? How can you tell what mint your coins came from? Look for the mint mark on the head side of the coin. On the penny to the right, look at the the "D" under the date. That indicates that this coin comes from the Denver mint. On other coins a tiny "P" refers to the Philadelphia mint. Older coins might have an "O" or an "S". They came from mints in New Orleans (O) and San Francisco (S), which are no longer in use. For more information about coins and mint marks go to the US Mint. Click Abe's head for the answer. Okay, we can if we eat quickly and we'll go out, okay? Although King Sejong the Great led the development of Hangeul to allow literacy to spread among common people and to create a writing system that represented the language more accurately than Hanja, it was not adopted by the upper classes of Koreans. Hanja would continue to be the official writing system until the late 19th century. Although it was rejected by the elite classes, Hangeul was used often by lower classes as a way to write down Korean literature and for lower classes to communicate. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Parent Child/SLI/PDF/dor36.cha = = = But she doesn't like them to be undressed which is funny 'cause some kids are really into the diaper thing. Title: How to Get Your Friend Back After a Facebook Fight Introduction: Facebook fights can be intense arguments or misunderstandings that occur between friends within the social media platform. These disputes may lead to strained relationships and even loss of friendship if not handled carefully. This wiki-style guide will provide you with in-depth steps and useful tips on how to mend your relationship with your friend after such an incident. Remember, patience and understanding are crucial throughout this process. Step 1: Cool Down and Reflect After a heated argument, give yourself some time to cool down before taking any action. Emotions often run high during these situations, making it difficult to communicate effectively. Use this time to reflect on what happened, identify triggers, and understand both sides of the story. By doing so, you'll approach the situation more calmly and rationally, increasing the chances of reconciliation. Key Tip: Avoid reacting immediately by sending messages or comments while still upset; they might escalate the conflict further. Step 2: Apologize Sincerely If you feel that you were at fault, apologize sincerely without expecting anything in return. A genuine apology shows maturity and respect towards your friend, which could help open up communication channels. Make sure to acknowledge your mistakes and express regret for hurting their feelings. An effective way to convey your apologies is through a private message rather than posting something public that might put additional pressure on them. Guideline: Be specific about what you're sorry for instead of using general statements like "I'm sorry for everything." This demonstrates that you have taken the time to think about the issue and genuinely want to make amends. Step 3: Listen Actively Once you've reached out, allow your friend to share their perspective without interruption. Active listening involves paying full attention to what they're saying, asking clarifying questions when needed, and validating their emotions. By showing empathy and understanding, you create a safe space for healing and forgiveness. Key Tip: Refrain from becoming defensive or dismissive of their feelings. Instead, focus on acknowledging their pain points and appreciating their willingness to communicate openly. Step 4: Seek Resolution Together Work together to find common ground and establish boundaries moving forward. Collaborative problem solving encourages compromise and mutual agreement, reducing the likelihood of future disagreements. During this conversation, avoid blaming or criticizing one another. Rather, emphasize positive communication strategies and agree on ways to maintain a healthy friendship. Guideline: Consider setting up regular check-ins or creating a code word to signal when conversations become too emotional or unproductive. Step 5: Give It Time Rebuilding trust takes time, effort, and consistency. Continuously demonstrate your commitment to rekindling the friendship by engaging in meaningful activities, communicating regularly, and being supportive. Over time, your actions will show your friend that you are genuinely interested in restoring the bond you once shared. Key Tip: Don't rush the process - allow it to unfold naturally as you continue working on improving your relationship. Conclusion: Repairing a friendship after a Facebook fight requires self-reflection, effective communication, collaboration, and patience. By following these steps and maintaining a positive attitude, you increase the probability of reconciling with your friend and strengthening the foundation of your relationship. Keep in mind that every situation is unique, so tailor these guidelines according to your specific circumstances. [dad repeats the rhyme Ross continues laughing] I Can Read On My Own Students need to be able to read fluently and independently in order to become better readers. Students need to have a desire to read independently in order to be voluntary readers. This lesson is designed to promote voluntary readers. This lesson is designed to promote voluntary reading. Three ways that encourage voluntary reading are presented in this lesson. They are: students choose books on their own, integrating discussion groups in class, and silent reading time everyday. Booklist with suggested books for students. Have plenty of variety and many award winning books. Examples would be: The Polar Express by: Chris Van Allsburg, Joseph's Little Overcoat, and Where the Wild Things Are by: Maurice Sendak. Di and Mice and Pen Pals by Educational Insights. Rainbow Fish by Marcus Pfister 1.) Teacher will introduce lesson. Reading on you own can be so much fun. There are some things that you can do to make reading by yourself fun. The first thing we can do is choose books that interest us to read by ourselves. 2.) It is important when you read with the class or when you read by yourself to make sure that we are reading what the text says. (Review cross-checking). Teacher reads the beginning of Did and Mice. Teacher will read a sentence that has one key word mistaken with something similar. The sentence does not make sense. Ask the students why t he sentence didn't make sense. 3.) The first part of reading on your own is choosing your own book. I am going to show you how to choose a book t hat you would like to read. First, we have to make sure that the book that we choose is not too hard for us. We are going to be sure that we can read the book by using the "Two Finger Rule". We are going to read the first page and put up one finger each time we come to a word we do not know. If you have two or more fingers up when you are done reading the first page you need to pick a different book, that one is a little too hard. Teacher models using Pen Pals and holds up three fingers for the three words that she did not know. This book is too hard for me. I will have to try this other one. Teacher models reading with the first page of Di and Mice and only has one finger in the air when she is done. I only have one finger in the air so this book is okay for me to read. 4.) Now let's go to the library and find out how to choose a book that we would enjoy. A very important part of reading is to do it because it is fun. When you are looking for a book anywhere, it is important to make sure you think that you are going to like the book. Sometimes a book will have a summary on the back or inside the flap. We can read that summary to see if we think we would be interested in the book. Other times, we should think about the topic of the book. Teacher will hold up Rainbow Fish. If you do not want to read a story about a fish then you should not pick this book. Do not look at the pictures to tell if that is the book you want to read. Pictures are like icing they make the cake taste better. 5.) While the students are in the library each one is given a chance to choose a book on their own which will be located within their reading level. 6.) Teacher will provide students with silent reading time on a daily basis. Both the teacher and the students should be reading during this time. The teacher will assess students by asking them questions about plot, character, and resolution. Students will also meet once a week to work in their discussion groups. They will do a book talk for each book they have read that week as well as discuss any books that they group may have read together. 7.) To assess whether children are reading independently, teacher will introduce Reading Logs. Student will keep track of the reading that they do on their own. Children will keep track of the number of pages they read, having a parent monitor and initial each day's reading. Wilson, P. (1992). Title: Delete a File Using Command Prompt **Introduction:** Deleting files using the command prompt can be an efficient way to manage your files, especially when you need to delete multiple files or files located in different directories. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting a file using the Windows Command Prompt. **Step 1: Open Command Prompt** Press `Win + R` to open the Run dialog box. Type `cmd` and press Enter. Alternatively, you can search for 'Command Prompt' in the Start menu. *Key Tip:* You can also open Command Prompt with administrative privileges by searching for 'Command Prompt' in the Start menu, right-clicking on the result, and selecting 'Run as administrator'. This is useful when you want to delete system or program files. **Step 2: Navigate to the Directory Containing the File** Use the `cd` (change directory) command to navigate to the directory containing the file you want to delete. For example, if the file is located in the C:\Users\YourName\Documents directory, type `cd C:\Users\YourName\Documents` and press Enter. *Key Guideline:* Always double-check the path before hitting enter, as typing incorrect paths could lead you to unintended locations. **Step 3: Verify the File Location** Once you are in the correct directory, list all files in that folder to verify whether the file is present there. Use the `dir` command to do this. If the file is listed, proceed to the next step; otherwise, retrace your steps with the `cd ..` command until you find the correct location. *Key Tip:* The `dir` command supports various parameters like sorting files based on size, date modified etc. To view these options, simply type `dir /?`. **Step 4: Delete the File** Now that you have confirmed the presence of the file, use the `del` command followed by the filename to delete it. For instance, if the file is named 'example.txt', type `del example.txt` and press Enter. *Important Note:* Deleted files go into the Recycle Bin from where they can be recovered unless emptied. However, certain commands such as `del /f` or `del /q`, which we discuss later, may permanently delete files without sending them to the Recycle Bin. Exercise caution while using these switches. Here are some additional switches you might find helpful when deleting files via Command Prompt: - `/p` : Pauses after each file confirmation message. It asks you to confirm if you really wish to delete that particular file. - `/s` : Deletes specified files along with their matching counterparts in all subdirectories. - `/q` : Quiet mode - Confirmation messages are suppressed during the deletion operation. - `/a[:][...]` : Used to switch between displaying hidden and non-hidden files. Syntax: `attrib +h Filename` hides a file whereas `attrib -h Filename` makes it visible again. Remember, understanding what each switch does before implementing it ensures safety and efficiency. Misuse could potentially cause loss of important data. **Conclusion:** This tutorial provided a comprehensive overview of how to delete a file using Command Prompt. By following these steps meticulously and being aware of the potential risks associated with advanced switches, users should now feel confident managing their files through this powerful tool. This project will help you understand the fundamentals of how web browsers work, as well as give you hands-on experience with Python programming. In this tutorial, we'll walk through creating a simple yet functional text-based web browser using Python 3. ### Part 1: Setting up your development environment **Step 1:** Install Python 3 Before starting, ensure that you have Python 3 installed on your computer. You can download it from the official website: <https://www.python.org/downloads/> After installation, open a command prompt or terminal window and type `python --version` to check if it is properly set up. The output should display the version number of your Python installation, such as 'Python 3.9.2'. **Step 2:** Set up a code editor (optional) While not required, using a dedicated code editor like Visual Studio Code, PyCharm, or Atom can make writing and managing your code easier. Download and install one of these editors if preferred. ### Part 2: Creating the basic structure of the text-based web browser **Step 1:** Import necessary libraries Open a new file in your chosen code editor or text editor and add the following lines at the beginning to import essential libraries: ```python import socket import re He didn't seem interested in the books or... Not tell him that she loved Francis Levison, or that he had spoken to her as he did; she valued her husband too greatly to draw him into any unpleasantness whose end could not be seen; but own to him that she had once felt a passing fancy for Francis Levison, and preferred not to be subjected to his companionship now. We've been to the zoo once or twice but um... Step 1 - Examine a Piece of Poor WritingFirst, use the overhead projector to show your class an example of typical (i.e. boring) grade level writing. Either use writing samples from previous years of teaching or ask another teacher for examples. It is not recommended to use samples from current students. After reading the piece together, ask the students to describe what they noticed about this piece of writing. The will likely tear it to shreds with valid criticisms! Then, ask the students to raise their hands if they think they sometimes tend to write like this. Most often, every single child raises his or her hand. Now they know what not to do. Step 2 - Show an Example of Strong WritingNext, show them an example of exemplary grade level writing, emphasizing that this student was the same age as them and that they could write like this, too. The kids can then point out all of the amazing techniques this student employed to make his/her writing pack a powerful punch! Show the students how to notice "magic words" that help paint a vivid picture in a reader's mind. Explain to your students that the point of writing is to make your reader see the same exact picture that you had in your head when you wrote the words. So, if you just mention a "dog," how are they going to know the color, size, and personality of the dog you envisioned if you don't tell them specifically? Use this anticipatory set to build your students' confidence and convince them that they too can, and will, write like this soon. Use Visualization to Make a PointThis next step seems so simple and works so effectively. First, choose a topic for this lesson's creative writing assignment. In my classroom, I use a jungle, but you can use anything that is familiar and interesting to the children. Ask the kids to close their eyes and visualize themselves standing in a particular landscape. Have them look around and notice what's above them, at their feet, behind them, on all sides. What's moving? What is in the background? What colors do they see? What small things and large things do they see? What do they hear, smell, feel, taste? What mood are they in? The more details you solicit, the better this visualization will work. After the details of the visualization are solidified in their minds, ask them to open their eyes and describe what they saw. Write their responses on a blank overhead or on the board. Coach the students to add "magic words" to their descriptions. Eliminate boring words such as "good," "bad," and "went." Help them mold their sentences into active, energetic works of art. After the first sentence or two, the kids should start doing this more on their own. As an example, this is what my kids came up with when I did this exercise with them: In the Wild Jungle Afterwards, read the descriptions back to the class. It is often stunning how powerful their writing can become in less than an hour! Then go back and analyze what makes this writing awesome and circle the magic words that they had chosen all on their own. To solidify the impact of this lesson, heap on the praise, saying how you knew they always had this kind of powerful writing in them and how you can't wait to put this up on the classroom web site for their families to see. Tell them that, now, they have no excuse but to produce more powerful and magical writing - writing that's just as exemplary as the outstanding writer's piece we had marveled at earlier. After you teach this lesson a few times, your students' writing, as a whole, will skyrocket to new heights. Each time, before they write, lead them in visualizing the scene of the story which helps them add details to their sentences. The students will strive to increase their vocabulary so that they can use more and more magical words in their work. Consider banning certain words (good, bad, said, went) and brainstorm magic words to replace these boring old standbys. Hang the new words around the room for the kids to pull from as they write. Set aside a place in the room where they can share "sparkling sentences" from the books they read during silent reading time. Even the most hesitant writers can't wait to share their writing with the class so that they can hear the class's sincere "oohs" and "aahs" as the audience visualizes the unique worlds they create on paper.
