The late 1960s and early 1970s saw a new generation of creative talent emerge at Marvel. In 1967 Jim Steranko began to write and draw stories featuring secret agent Nick Fury in the anthology book "Strange Tales". Steranko was influenced in his work by James Bond films and the psychedelic and Op art movements, and the resulting stories melded groundbreaking visuals with equally innovative storytelling techniques. Writer Chris Claremont and artist John Byrne began a long collaboration on "The Uncanny X-Men" in 1975. The pair revitalized the flagging series with characters such as Wolverine and complex story arcs that soon made the X-Men franchise one of Marvel's best sellers. Title: How to Be Popular in 7th Grade (for Guys) =============================================== Introduction ------------ Middle school can be a challenging time as you navigate new social dynamics and strive to fit in. Being popular in 7th grade isn't just about having the most friends; it's about being respected, liked, and influential among your peers. This guide will provide actionable steps and valuable insights to help you become more popular in 7th grade. Keep in mind that everyone's experience is unique, so focus on being true to yourself while implementing these strategies. ### Step 1: Develop Good Hygiene and Personal Grooming **Explanation:** First impressions matter, and taking care of your appearance sends a message that you respect yourself and others. Plus, good hygiene reduces the spread of germs and illnesses. * **Key Tips:** + Shower daily and wash your hair regularly. + Brush and floss your teeth twice a day. + Use deodorant or antiperspirant. + Wear clean clothes and ensure they are appropriately sized. + Style your hair neatly. ### Step 2: Improve Communication Skills **Explanation:** Effective communication skills enable you to express yourself clearly, listen actively, and build strong relationships with your classmates. * **Key Tips:** + Practice active listening by focusing on the speaker and asking relevant questions. + Speak confidently and clearly without mumbling or speaking too softly. + Avoid using filler words like "um" and "like." yeah, we're going to eat 'em and decorate 'em tomorrow and then eat 'em tomorrow too. Return to Physics Index Sandra Tucker Crown Fine Arts Academy 2128 S. St. Louis Chicago IL 60623 This lesson is designed for primary level students. They will observe the transfer of momentum. 2 or more pairs of rollerblades 2 cars - each car has a spring attached to the front bumper and velcro attached to the rear bumper. a weight (that will fit on one of the cars) Begin with a roller blade demonstration. Tell the students they will need to observe what happens. Students of approximately the same weight will put on rollerblades and will participate in four demonstrations. (Be sure students wearing roller blades are also wearing helmets and other protective gear.) l. One person stands still. The other person skates into him. At the point when they would collide, they push off from each other. 2. Repeat above, but this time, as they meet they grab on to each other. 3. Both students skate towards each other. When they meet, they push off from each other. 4. Repeat above, but this time, they grab on to each other. This entire series can be repeated using one heavier student (or two students together) and one lighter student. See if the results are the same. If possible, let all students try these demonstrations with a partner. Now repeat the demonstration using the 2 cars. The students will observe and discuss what they see. The teacher can draw the diagrams of the car's movement on the board. 1. One car moving - one car still. The springs on the cars are facing each other. Observation: The moving car stops and the still car moves in the same direction the moving car was going. 2. One car moving - one car still. The velcro on the cars is facing each other. Observation: The cars stick together and move slowly in the direction the moving car was going. 3. Both cars moving towards each other. The springs on the cars are facing each other. Observation: Each car moves in the opposite direction but slower. 4. Both cars moving towards each other. The velcro on the cars is facing each other. Observation: The cars stop. These demonstrations can be repeated putting a weight on one of the cars. Some of the observations will differ. Students should observe that movement transfers from one car to the other. This is called the transfer of momentum. They should also see that the weight of the car (or other object) affects the speed and direction of the movement. Now do a demonstration using cue balls. Line up two cue balls. Have a student hit them with a cue stick so they move in an angle. Use a string to show where the ball was hit and the path each ball takes. The students should be able to see a 90 degree angle. Ask them if they see an "L". Repeat several times. Now give the students a bag of different size marbles and string and let them try to see what kinds of angles are made when you hit two marbles together. Have them observe if the angle changes when the marbles are the same compared to when the marbles are different. Discuss the observations with the class. Students should be able to describe what "transfer" means, when discussing transfer of momentum. In any neutral atom, the number of electrons is the same as the number of protons. As a result, isotopes of the same element also have the same number of electrons and the same electronic structure. The way an atom acts is decided by its electronic structure, so isotopes of the same element have almost the same chemical behavior, such as the molecules it can form. It is very difficult to separate the isotopes of an element from a mixture of different isotopes because of how similar this behavior is. Did you do it all by yourself? The average rate of change of a population is the total change divided by the time taken for that change to occur. The average rate of change can be calculated with only the times and populations at the beginning and end of the period. Calculating the average rate of change is similar to calculating the average velocity of an object, but is different from calculating the instantaneous rate of change. I want to talk about average rate of change. We've talked about average velocity. Average rate of change is a similar concept only we'll be applying it to functions that don't measure position. They'll measure something else. Let's take a look at an example. The population F of T of gnomes in Thuringia is described by the table below. T is the year. And so we have the years from 1850 to 1900, skipping by tens, and the population starting at 3200 and ending at 22,800. This is how we define average rate of change of F of T over an interval. It's F of B minus F of A over by minus A. Looks like average velocity. It's really the same kind of thing. It's the average rate of change. Let's calculate the rate of change of the gnome population on this interval between 1850 and 1880. So 1850 is going to be our A value. 1880 will be our B value. And so we'll have to compute F of 1880 minus F of 1850. Over 1880 minus 1850. Okay. So let's look at our table. In 1880, the population of gnomes was 12 -- rather 10,400. 1850 it was 3200. So 10,400. And 3200. That's divided by-- this is a 30-year difference. 30. 10,400 minus 3,200 is 7,200, and that's in gnomes. Gotta remember units. Because actually when you're dealing with average rate of change, the units will tell you, they'll tell you what the average rate of change actually means. So this is 30 years. And so let's simplify this answer. 72 divided by 3 is 24. So this would be 240 gnomes per year. What this means is -- now you notice that the population of gnomes has increased by 7200 in this 30-year period. 240 gnomes per year is how fast the gnome population would have to grow if it were growing at a constant rate in order to make up that increase. So 240 gnomes per year. Now let's look at the rate of change of the population from 1880 to 1900. That's going to be F of 1900 minus F of 1880 over 1900 minus 1880. So always remember, it's final population minus initial population. Or final quantity minus initial quantity, whatever the quantity is that F measures. So, again, we look at our table. At 1900, we had 22,800 gnomes. 800 gnomes. 10,400 in 1880. So 22.8. 22,800, 10,400 and this is going to be a 20-year period. So 20. So we have 22 minus 10, 12. 8 minus 4, 4. 12,400 over 20. So let me cancel the 0 here. This is going to be 620 gnomes, right, that's the units at the top, gnomes per year. Okay. So the average rate of growth of the population from 1880 to 1900 is 620 gnomes per year. Average rate of change is exactly like average velocity. It's the change in the quantity F divided by the change in time. Do you wanna height get the ball? Could I have some Daniel, thank you? That's the thing in front of her. Oh, oh, it hurt my hair XXX. The scale that I am going to be teaching you today is the B Flat minor melodic scale. Like all melodic scales, this one changes the notes as you come back down the scale. The notes on the way up are B flat, C, D flat, E flat, F, G, A and back to B flat. Following the B flat, you begin to walk down with the notes A flat, G flat, F, E flat, D flat, C, and back to B flat. The fingering for the right hand begins with the second finger on the B flat. The thumb comes under to hit the C, then the second and third fingers hit the D flat and E flat. The thumb comes under again and hits the F. The scale climb finishes with the fourth finger on the B flat. Coming down from the B flat your third finger is going to hit the A flat, your second finger the G flat, and your thumb the F. Now you simply cross your third finger over and finish with the same notes and fingering that you climbed up with. On the left hand, start with the second finger on the B flat, cross over with the fourth finger to the D flat, then continue up the scale. When the thumb gets to the G, the third finger crosses over to the A and you finish the climb with the second finger on the B flat. Coming back down, hit the A flat with your third finger, cross your thumb under to hit the G flat, then follow the scale back down using the same notes and fingering that you used coming up. Next, you can practice the B Major Scale. Getting into the Port Orleans Walt Disney World Spirit Port Orleans is one of the many themed resorts at Walt Disney World Resort in Florida. This resort is made up of two separate areas: French Quarter and Riverside. Both areas offer a unique New Orleans-style atmosphere that will make you feel as if you've been transported to The Big Easy. Here's how to get into the Port Orleans Walt Disney World spirit: Step 1: Learn About Port Orleans Before you can fully embrace the Port Orleans spirit, it's important to understand its history and themes. Research the resort online or read guidebooks to learn about its layout, architecture, and backstory. Understanding the attention to detail that went into creating this immersive environment will help you appreciate your stay even more. Key Tip: Check out the official Walt Disney World website for an overview of the resort, including dining options, recreational activities, and transportation services. Step 2: Dress Appropriately To truly immerse yourself in the Port Orleans experience, dress in attire reminiscent of old Louisiana. Think seersucker suits, straw hats, and linen dresses. For Mardi Gras celebrations, consider dressing up in festive masks and beads. Guideline: While costumes are allowed, avoid wearing anything too elaborate or disruptive. Always adhere to Walt Disney World's costume policy. Step 3: Explore the Area Take some time to wander around the resort and take in all the details. From the ironwork balconies to the gas lamps lining the streets, there's so much to see and discover. Make sure to visit both French Quarter and Riverside to compare their distinct atmospheres. Key Tip: Take advantage of the boat transportation between Port Orleans and Disney Springs for additional shopping and dining opportunities. Step 4: Participate in Activities Join in on the various recreational activities offered at Port Orleans. Go fishing, rent a bike, or try your hand at horse-drawn carriage rides. During Mardi Gras season, don't miss the nightly street party with live music, floats, and bead tossing. Guideline: Some activities may require advance reservations or have age restrictions. Be sure to check with guest services for specific activity information. Step 5: Embrace Southern Hospitality The staff at Port Orleans strives to provide exceptional service with a touch of southern charm. Engage with cast members and other guests by using polite manners and addressing others respectfully. A simple "please" and "thank you" go a long way! Step 6: Try Traditional NOLA Cuisine No trip to Port Orleans would be complete without indulging in some classic New Orleans dishes. Enjoy jambalaya, gumbo, crawfish touff e, or beignets at one of the resort's restaurants or food stands. Key Tip: Visit Sassagoula Floatworks and Food Factory for quick service options featuring Creole and Cajun favorites. Step 7: Attend Special Events Throughout the year, Port Orleans hosts various special events such as holiday parties, cooking demonstrations, and wine tastings. These events often sell out quickly, so book early to secure your spot. Guideline: Keep an eye on the Walt Disney World website or subscribe to their email newsletter for updates on upcoming events at Port Orleans. By following these steps, you'll be well on your way to embracing the magic and wonder of Port Orleans Walt Disney World Resort. Laissez les bon temps rouler (let the good times roll)! In some countries, like the United States of America, Saturday is the seventh and final day of the week. In other parts of the world, like the United Kingdom, Saturday is said to be the sixth day of the week. It is also the day of rest and worship for Jewish people. Along with Sunday, it makes up the weekend. Its name comes from the Roman god Saturn. What did you do at school today? He's going to go back inside the pipe and run to the other end. On the other side of the piano. Let your child express negative feelings without judging them. Imagine if every time you were upset, some bigger, taller, frowning person looked down at you and said, 'Don't feel that way' or 'Don't tell me that'. Would you feel like shutting up or shouting back? Ask yourself, 'Am I really listening to my child?' Or are you waiting to tell your child what you think? Children often start to have a tantrum because they don't feel heard. 'If you are thinking of what you will say while your child is talking, then you know you are not really listening', advises Michael Thompson, PhD. Reflect your child's feelings. For example, you might say, 'Wow. You're really cranky' to a younger child. To a school-age child, you might try, 'I can see how frustrated you are. Can you tell me what made you feel that way?' ('What' is always more important than 'why' - it asks for specifics.) Slow down the process. For example, you could say, 'I need a moment to think about this'. If your child is being rude, or getting ready to have a tantrum, you can slow things down by giving feedback. You might say, 'Ouch! That comment hurts my feelings' or 'I can see you're upset. Let's talk'. Use this opportunity to problem-solve. If kids are fighting, you might say, 'In this family (or house) we don't hurt people's feelings. Let's try to solve this problem another way'. Then ask the kids for their ideas of what would be fair. You might say, 'You don't think it's fair that you have to go to bed before your sister. I understand. What do you think should happen?' Ask your child to explain it again. Even if you disagree, you might say, 'Explain to me again why it feels so unfair'. This requires a child to settle down and articulate feelings. Acknowledge your child's effect on you. Many children will calm down if you acknowledge their impact - and get angrier if you don't. You might stop and say something like, 'I've stopped the car ... (or 'I am off the phone') and you have my full attention'. Then ask questions like, 'What don't I understand?' Focus on your child's behaviour, not your child's character. You might say, 'Yelling in the kitchen is not OK right now', instead of, 'How many times do I have to tell you to stop yelling?' Discuss the consequences of your child's behaviour. You might say, 'Running inside can be dangerous. You might slip or knock something over'. This can be more effective than saying, 'Get out of the kitchen'. Set limits that your child will find comforting. A limit is not a punishment. Limits can help children learn how to calm themselves down. 'Kids find the setting of limits comforting and soothing', comments John Gottman. 'They need to know that you (the parent) are in control.' Make consequences relevant - and explain them. 'Make the punishment fit the crime. If a kid spills milk (on purpose), the child has to help clean it up, not get a time out', says Gillian McNamee, PhD. Give everyone a turn to complain If your kids are fussing and whining, instead of saying 'Don't whine', set a formal time for complaining (but put a time limit on it). This way, you can all share, vent and get it out. Give everyone in the family a turn. Be empathetic and try to listen beyond the whining.' - John Gottman, PhD, author of Raising an emotionally intelligent child You're workin' really hard to figure that out, huh? Now it was ready for the wind to make it nice and clean. Systole (when the heart squeezes) happens because the muscle cells of the heart gets smaller in size. When they get smaller we also say they "contract". Electricity going through the heart makes the cells contract. The electricity starts in the "sino-atrial node" (acronym SA Node) The SA Node is a group of cells in the right atria. These cells start an "electrical impulse". This electrical impulse sets the rate and timing at which all cardiac muscle cells contract. This motion is called 'atrial systole'. Once electrical impulse goes through the "atrio-ventricular node" (AV Node). The AV Node makes the impulse slow down. Slowing down makes the electrical impulses get to the ventricles later. That is what makes the ventricular systole occur "after" atrial systole, and lets all the blood leave the atria before ventricle contracts ("meaning squeeze"). However, there may be situations where interactions with law enforcement officers occur. This tutorial will provide you with steps to avoid dealing with the police while taking pictures in public and ensure your rights are respected. Step 1: Familiarize Yourself with Local Laws and Regulations Before heading out to take photos, research local laws regarding photography and filming in public spaces. Some cities or states might have specific regulations that restrict certain types of photography or require permits. Understanding these rules can help prevent potential conflicts with authorities. * Key Tip: Check resources like the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) website for information about photographers' rights in your area. Step 2: Choose Appropriate Locations Select locations for your photoshoot carefully. While it is legal to take photographs in most public places, some areas such as airports, military bases, or government buildings may have stricter restrictions. Steer clear of sensitive sites to minimize the chances of encountering law enforcement. Step 3: Be Respectful and Aware of Your Surroundings Always maintain awareness of your environment and those around you. Being respectful to others and avoiding confrontational behavior can reduce tensions and lower the likelihood of drawing unwanted attention from the police. * Key Guideline: Refrain from using tripods, lighting equipment, or other gear that could obstruct pedestrian traffic or create safety hazards. Step 4: Dress appropriately for the Situation Dressing casually and blending in with your surroundings can make you less noticeable as a photographer. Avoid wearing clothing that explicitly identifies you as a member of the press or a professional photographer if possible. Step 5: Use Discretion When Necessary If you feel uncomfortable taking pictures openly, consider using discretion. Utilizing smaller cameras, lenses, or shooting from the hip can draw less attention to yourself. Additionally, capturing candid moments rather than posed shots may also decrease the chance of being approached by the police. Step 6: Maintain Professionalism During Interactions In case you do encounter law enforcement while taking photographs, remain calm and courteous. Clearly communicate your intentions and assert your rights politely but firmly. Remember that cooperation does not mean relinquishing your constitutional protections. * Key Tip: If an officer asks you to stop taking photos, ask if you are legally required to comply. If they cannot provide a valid reason, continue documenting the interaction peacefully. Step 7: Know Your Rights Understand your rights as a photographer in public spaces. Generally, you have the right to take photographs of anything visible from a public vantage point without permission. However, this may vary depending on location and circumstances. * Key Guideline: Learn the difference between private property (where consent is usually needed) and public space (where photography is typically allowed). Conclusion: By following these steps and maintaining situational awareness, you can significantly reduce the likelihood of interacting with law enforcement while practicing your passion for photography in public settings. Always remember your rights and act professionally during any encounters with officials to ensure a positive experience. [Mary is coming down to the car] Maybe those bolts [nuts] are too heavy. Imagine you are part of a club where there are only ten members, including yourself. This club has a special rule - every time you all meet, you must stand in a line, but no one can stand in the same place as they did during the last meeting. Over time, you start to notice that after a while, some people will end up back in their original spots, even though you're all trying to find new places to stand each time! Let's say your friend Sarah always stands in the first spot when you form a line. According to the club rule, she cannot return to that position until everyone else has had a turn standing there since her last visit. Since there are nine other people besides Sarah, this means Sarah needs to wait for them to take turns before going back to the beginning. Now let's think about another member named Tim who initially stood in the second spot. Just like Sarah, he too has to wait for everyone else to occupy his previous position before returning to it. Given that there are ten people in total, how many times could Tim go around the circle before reaching the exact same configuration again? The answer would be less than or equal to ten because once Tim completes a full cycle through all possible positions, he must be back at his starting point. In general, whenever any person in this club returns to their initial position, the whole arrangement of people along the line goes back to its original state. So, by extension, the maximum number of unique arrangements you can create within this group is limited by the size of the group itself. That's why we say that for a prime number $p$, the equation ${x^m}\equiv {1}\ (\bmod{\ p})$ can have at most $m$ distinct solutions among the numbers $1, 2, ..., p-1$. Beyond those $m$ solutions, any additional attempts to rearrange the sequence simply lead us back to previously explored configurations. Pit eyes are set in a depression in the skin. This reduces the angles at which light can enter. It allows the organism to say where the light is coming from. But this will stop you from turning pages. Title: Understanding Simple Recursion with the Chocolate Box Example Have you ever heard of recursion? It's like looking at a repeating pattern or solving a puzzle step by step. Let me tell you a fun story to understand this concept using chocolates! Imagine you have a big box of chocolates. Your task is to write down how many pieces of chocolate there are in total when some friends come over and take turns grabbing a handful from the box. But here's the catch - every time someone takes a turn, they always pick up exactly 'c' number of chocolates. You don't know how many chocolates your friends will scoop up each time, but you do know that it'll never be more than 'n'. Also, there are at least two chocolates taken out per friend, so no one leaves empty-handed. Now let's think about our strategy: 1. **Base Case**: When there are absolutely no chocolates left (which means z=0), we obviously have counted all the chocolates already. So, the number of chocolates returned should be zero. Makes sense, doesn't it? _If z equals 0, return 0._ 2. **Recursive Assumption**: Assume that we have a magical function called `countChocolates()` which correctly tells us the remaining amount after any previous friend took their share (as long as they didn't take more than 'n', grabbed at least 2 chocolates, and there were initially 'y' chocolates). 3. **Breaking Down The Problem**: Suppose another friend comes along and grabs some chocolates (let's say 'c'). To find the current count of chocolates, we need to consider the following steps: * Calculate how many full sets of 'c' chocolates could fit in the leftover pile (let's name this part 'A') by calling our imaginary helper function `countChocolates()`. * Then, see how many extra ('leftover') chocolates remain once those complete sets are removed (we'll label this bit 'B'), and add these to the next friend's share. * Finally, combine both results to get the new total (_i.e.,_ `totalChocolates = A + B`). Let's put it together now: Suppose you start with 'y' chocolates, and another friend picks up 'c'. Our goal is to calculate the remaining chocolates, knowing that the friend picked up less than or equal to 'n': * Call the magical `countChocolates(yc, n)` (where 'yc' represents the first friend taking 'c' candies). This breaks down further into two cases: + Remaining chocolates after picking full sets (calculated as 'A'): `countChocolates((y/c)*c, n)`, and + Extra candies not forming full sets (calculated as 'B'): `yc mod c`. * Combine them to determine the updated total of chocolates: `countChocolates(yc, n) = A + B`. And voila! With this approach, even though we don't know precisely how many chocolates our pals take during their visit, we can still accurately keep track of the final tally! Just like unwrapping delicious layers of chocolates while working through our problem step-by-step, recursion allows us to tackle complex situations without getting overwhelmed-one assumption and calculation at a time. Title: How to Perform a Neat Handstand Performing a neat handstand requires strength, balance, and coordination. It may take time and practice to master, but with patience and persistence, you can do it! This tutorial will guide you through the process, providing detailed steps, helpful tips, and important safety guidelines. **Step 1: Warm Up** Warming up is crucial before attempting any physical activity. Spend at least five minutes doing light cardio exercises like jumping jacks or jogging in place. Follow this with stretching, focusing on your shoulders, wrists, and core muscles. A proper warm-up increases blood flow, reduces injury risk, and prepares your body for exercise. **Step 2: Practice Wall Walks** Wall walks help build upper body strength necessary for handstands. Stand about two feet away from a wall. Place both hands on the ground, then walk them towards the wall while walking your feet up the wall simultaneously until your body forms an L shape. Hold for a few seconds, then reverse the movement by walking your hands out and feet down. Repeat several times daily. **Step 3: Perfect Your Cartwheel** Being able to perform a clean cartwheel is essential for learning a handstand. Make sure you're kicking off with the correct foot (dominant leg back, non-dominant leg forward) and landing safely. Practicing cartwheels also enhances your spatial awareness, which assists in maintaining balance during a handstand. **Step 4: Try Kicking Up into a Handstand** Stand facing a wall. Bend one knee and lift that leg, preparing to kick off. Swiftly push off the standing leg as you extend the bent leg into the air, aiming to land your heels against the wall. Spotting - fixing your gaze on a point straight ahead - can aid balance. If successful, hold the position briefly before lowering yourself gently. **Step 5: Master the Tuck Position** The tuck position involves bringing your knees close to your chest when inverted. From the kicked-up handstand position, bend your legs at the hips and pull them towards your torso. Keep practicing this motion; once comfortable, progress to Step 6. **Step 6: Transition to Straddle Position** Gradually straighten your legs while keeping them together, moving from the tucked position to straddling your arms. Engage your core throughout this transition to maintain stability. Remember to breathe steadily. **Step 7: Refine Your Balance** Balance becomes critical as you move further away from supporting walls. To improve balancing skills, try performing handstands near a spotter who can provide minor adjustments if needed. Over time, reduce reliance on external support. **Key Tips:** * Always prioritize form over height. Proper alignment ensures safety and effectiveness. * Use mats or soft surfaces to cushion falls. * Progress gradually. Don't rush the learning process to avoid injuries. * Regularly engage in overall fitness activities to enhance stamina and flexibility. **Guidelines:** * Consult a physician before starting new workout routines, especially if you have existing health conditions. * Never attempt advanced moves without adequate preparation. * Train under supervision whenever possible, particularly during initial stages. * Listen to your body. Pain indicates something isn't right; stop immediately and seek advice if necessary. A couple from books and real life. He's afraid of the bugs that are outside? The "Theatre of the Absurd" plays have some of the same ideas that are found in the philosophy (a way of thinking) called existentialism. Existentialism is very different from many other "philosophies". Most religions and philosopies say that human life has a meaning (or a purpose). The philosophy of existentialism is that human life does not have a meaning (or a purpose). When something has no meaning, it is "absurd". (absurd means means silly and meaningless.) Choosing the right crutches is an important decision that can significantly impact your mobility, comfort, and overall well-being while recovering from an injury or surgery. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of selecting the best crutches for your needs, with clear explanations, helpful tips, and practical advice at every step. Step 1: Consult Your Healthcare Provider Before choosing crutches, consult your healthcare provider, physical therapist, or occupational therapist. They can provide personalized recommendations based on your medical history, current condition, and rehabilitation goals. Following their guidance will ensure proper support during your recovery. Step 2: Measure Correctly for Crutch Size Accurate measurements are crucial for comfortable and safe crutch usage. To determine the correct crutch size, stand upright wearing shoes you typically wear. Have someone else measure from the ground to your armpits while keeping your arms relaxed by your sides. The ideal height should have a 1.5 to 2 inches (3.8 to 5 cm) gap between your underarm and the top of the crutch. If there's too much space, consider using lower crutch settings; if there's not enough room, opt for taller ones. Step 3: Select the Appropriate Type of Crutches There are two main types of crutches: axillary (underarm) and forearm (Lofstrand). Axillary crutches offer more stability but may cause discomfort under the arms over time. Forearm crutches require some upper body strength but distribute weight evenly across the forearms and hands. Choose the type that suits your abilities, injury, and preferences. Step 4: Assess Handle Comfort and Adjustability Ensure handles fit securely into your hands without causing strain or slippage. Look for ergonomic designs that reduce pressure points and fatigue. Handles must be adjustable so you can maintain a natural wrist position - ideally, they should align with your wrists when gripping them. Test different grip styles to find what feels most comfortable and supportive. Step 5: Evaluate Crutch Tips for Traction and Durability Crutch tips play a vital role in ensuring safety and preventing falls. Opt for high-quality rubber tips offering excellent traction on various surfaces. Check for signs of wear or damage before purchasing new crutches. Replace worn-out tips promptly to maintain optimal performance and avoid accidents. Step 6: Consider Additional Features Based on Individual Needs Some users might benefit from specific features such as armrests, padding, or built-in baskets. For example, armrests can provide extra support and comfort for those who struggle with balance or endurance. Padding along the shaft can alleviate pressure points and prevent bruising. Built-in baskets or pouches offer convenient storage solutions for essential items like phones, keys, or water bottles. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always prioritize function and safety over aesthetics when choosing crutches. * Avoid leaning heavily on axillary crutches, which could lead to nerve damage or other complications. * Practice good posture while using crutches, maintaining a straight back and equal weight distribution. * Regularly check crutch components for any signs of wear or damage and replace parts as needed. * Seek professional assistance if experiencing pain, discomfort, or difficulty maneuvering with crutches. By following this thorough guide and considering individual needs and preferences, you'll be well-equipped to choose the perfect pair of crutches for a smooth and successful recovery journey. Exploit the natural cycle The bulbs that are suited to forcing have a topsy-turvy cycle of growth in nature and in the garden. Most of them flower early in spring, while trees are still bare. Their leaves and roots persist just long enough-through mid- to late spring-for the bulbs to store a new supply of energy. Then the leaves and roots die and the bulbs go dormant for the summer and part of the fall. In fall, when the soil cools, the bulbs awaken and send out new roots, the first step in their preparation for the next spring. The roots continue to grow until the soil cools to 40F. In most of the United States, the bulbs produce a dense mat of roots before winter sets in. In cold climates, freezing temperatures halt the roots' growth but won't kill them; in warmer climates, the bulbs may continue to make roots throughout the winter. When spring temperatures rise to between 40F and 60F, bulbs reawaken, send up leaves, and then make flowers. This growth is rapid, thanks to the full complement of roots. Before the bulbs go dormant again by fall, the leaves have plenty of time to replenish the energy of the bulbs. Forcing exploits this cycle. In fall, you buy dormant bulbs, pot them, and keep them cold for several months to stimulate them to make roots. Once plants have roots, you bring the pots to a windowsill, where the warmth tricks the bulbs into thinking that spring has arrived. In short order, they flower, and you have an early spring. A parade of bulbs: Bulbs for forcing vary in shape and size. Shoots grow from the top; roots from the bottom. Plant bottom-end down. It is sometimes considered a post-transition metal. Its symbol on the periodic table is "Zn". Zinc is the 30th element on the periodic table, and has an atomic number of 30. Zinc has a mass number of 65.38. It contains 30 protons and 30 electrons. In total, 29 isotopes of zinc are known, and five of these occur in nature. Some isotopes are radioactive. This picture shows you where the light beams would be. Here are the materials I used. I used an HB pencil, mechanical pencil, 6B pencil, tortillion and a kneaded eraser. I also used a ruler but I forgot to put it in the picture. Now on to drawing. Draw the basic shapes of the 3D objects. The cube is a straight line, the sphere is a curved line, the cone is an angle, and the cylinder is two straight lines. Next, turn those lines into shapes. For the cube, draw a square. The sphere is a circle, the cone is a triangle, and the cylinder is two curves connected to two straight lines. Then draw actual 3D shapes. Draw a cube, sphere, cone and cylinder. I drew dotted lines to show you the other sides, faces and vertices just to help you. (You don't have to draw the dotted lines if you don't want to.) Now we are going to add in the tone of the cube. Tone is the overall lightness or darkness of an object. Shade in the front face and side face of the cube with an HB pencil. Then blend in the tone with a tortillion, or, if you don't have one, use a cotton ball or q-tip. Do the same thing to the cone and cylinder, but shade the whole thing. Also do the tone to the cylinder, but don't shade the top face. Now we are going to do the actual shading. All you have to do for the cube is shade the side face darker than the tone. Don't do anything to the top and front faces. Next, shade the cone and sphere. Start from the darkest on the left side and then gradually get lighter as you move toward the right side. Then erase the left over tone with your kneaded eraser. Then do the same thing to the cylinder, but don't do anything to the top face. Now we are going to draw the cast shadows. Cast shadows are shadows that appear on the side away from the light. With your 6B pencil, add in the shadows. Oooh, my favorite part! We are going to make these look like actual 3D objects. The cube is a block of wood. Use the ruler to draw lines about 1 cm apart. Then draw wood patterns inside them with the mechanical pencil. The sphere is a tennis ball, and the cone is like a tent thing. With the mechanical pencil, draw the two curves on the sphere and then little lines for hair. Then draw little lines at the top vertice on the cone and then draw the tent opening. Shade inside the opening with the 6B pencil. Then draw lines down the cone. The cylinder is a bucket. Draw lines going down it, and then draw curved lines. For the handle, connect another curved lines to the original curved lines. Here is what your 3D objects should look like when you are done. The Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) is the number of radiation pulses emitted by radar in 1 second. For example, if the radar emits 400 pulses in one second then the PRF is 400 pulses/second. Think of pulses like the pulses of a strobe light. A strobe light alternates between light and dark and there is light a certain number of times within a given period of time. Radar is similar except the number of pulses is much more per second than a strobe light and radar emits microwave type wavelength radiation. Another difference is that the radar spends less than 1% of the time emitting radiation and over 99% of the time sensing for returned radiation. Radar can sample the troposphere very fast because the speed of light is fast (about 300,000,000 meters per second). Rmax stands for the maximum range the radar can detect. If the radar emitted a pulse of energy and waited as long as needed for returning radiation then the radar could detect to any range. However, since the speed of light is so fast compared to the distances we need to measure returns in the troposphere the radar is not required to wait more than a tiny fraction of a second for return energy to come back. Thus, the radar can be set to emit and listen for 100s of pulses per second and we can still measure ranges that cover a broad area. However, the faster the PRF becomes the smaller of a range that can be detected. If the PRF is set too fast then there is not as much time to sample the troposphere in one pulse before the next pulse is sent out. Energy returned from one pulse after another pulse has been sent will be range folded. Suppose the PRF is 500 pulses per second. The formula for Rmax is C / (2 * PRF). C stands for the speed of light. With a PRF of 500 pulses/s, the Rmax is = 300,000,000 m/s / (1,000 s^-1) = 300,000 m which is equal to 300 km. Thus the radar can sample up to 300 km during each pulse. If a return is beyond 300 km then it will be range folded and will show up at a distance closer to the radar than the return really is because the radar thinks it is getting returns from a second pulse it already sent out. Suppose we increase the PRF to 1,200 pulses per second. Rmax then becomes 300,000,000 m/s / (2,400 s^-1) = 125 km. From these two examples you can see that as the PRF increases, then the Rmax becomes a smaller range. This makes sense because the faster pulses are emitted the less time there is for the pulse to travel and come back to the radar before the next pulse is emitted. If we want to detect echoes beyond 125 km we will need to decrease the PRF from 1,200 to a smaller number. When a reflectivity image is put into motion we can see where the precipitation areas are moving toward and how fast they are moving. However, we can not see the motions within the precipitation areas very well. To help with that problem Doppler radar has come along. Vmax stands for the maximum velocity the radar can detect. Precipitation particles that are sensed are either moving closer to the radar over time, further from the radar over time or stay the same distance from the radar over time. It is this motion we want to detect because from it the motions inside rain clouds can be sensed. The motion of precipitation particles or other particles is determined by the phase shift that occurs from radar radiation striking the particle. Suppose you throw a ball at a wall that is moving at you and then throw another ball at the same velocity at a wall that is moving away from you. The ball you threw at the wall that is moving toward you will rebound faster off the wall back toward you. Thus, the velocity of the ball changes relative to you depending on if the wall is moving further or closer to you even if you throw it at the same velocity toward the wall both times. This principle does not work with light because the speed of light is a constant. However, the frequency (number of light waves passing a point over time) and wavelength (distance from beginning to end of each wave) does change. It is from the phase shift of light that is used by radar to determine whether an object is moving toward or away from the radar and the magnitude of that motion. Suppose the PRF is 500 pulses per second using a 0.1 meter wavelength radar. The formula for Vmax = (PRF * wavelength) / 4. With a PRF of 500 pulses/s, the Vmax is = (500 s^-1 * 0.1 m) / 4 = 12.5 m/s. Thus the radar can only sample motions that are equal to or less than 12.5 m/s. If the actual velocity of an object is 17.5 m/s that echo will be velocity folded and will have a value of (17.5 - 12.5 = 5 m/s). If we want to detect higher velocities without them being folded the PRF needs to be increased. Suppose we increase the PRF to 1,200 pulses per second. Vmax becomes (1,200 s^-1 * 0.1 m) / 4 = 30 m/s. From these two examples you can see that as the PRF increases, then the Vmax becomes higher. Think of a strobe light once again and the strobe light shining on a bouncing ball. If the strobe light flickers more quickly (higher PRF) and we watch it in slow motion then we can predict where the ball will be each time the light shines on it again. However, if the pulses are longer (PRF decreased) to the point where the light shines again slower than the time it takes the ball to make one bounce we will not know whether the ball is rising or falling (it has folded and we can no longer be sure where it will be when the light shines on the ball on the next pulse). The phase shift of light is smaller the higher the PRF is. As the PRF decreases the phase shift becomes more. Once the phase shift becomes too much then the velocity will be folded. And we have a picture and so sometimes she sees it and she says [Pop starts to put a clothes pin in the pocket on the page] Introduce vocabulary: In the Small, Small Pond (Fleming, 1998) |Activity Type: Introduce| |Activity Form: Standard| |Grade: K, 1| |Group Size: Small Group, Whole Class| |Length: 30 minutes| |Materials: In the Small, Small Pond, Denise Fleming (ISBN 0805059830)| |Goal: Given a word, the student can say its meaning| |Items: doze, hover, plunge, scatter, swoop, wade (How to add vocabulary)| What to do - If more than three words are listed below, choose three. (It's tough for students to take in more than three new words in one go.) Write the three words on sentence strips (so they can easily be displayed after the lesson). - I'm going to read a new book to you today. It's called In the Small, Small Pond. It uses some words you might not know, so I'm going to tell you the words now. Then, when I read the story, I want you to raise your hand when you hear the word. Okay? - Tell students the three words and their meanings. Have them repeat the words back to you. - Remember, when you hear any of our three words, raise your hand. Ready? - Read the story. Praise students who correctly identify the words as you read. Repeat each word's meaning as you encounter it. - When you finish reading, go through the three words giving the complete sequence below for each word. You can give the examples/non-examples either to the whole group or to individual students. Feel free to add your own examples and non-examples, particularly if students seem unclear. - Optionally, read the story again. - Students need to encounter a word multiple times before learning it. So: - Try to find opportunities to use the three words during other activities in the next 24 hours. - Ask students to use the word themselves and praise them strongly when they do. - Be particularly excited about usage in contexts different from that in the book you read, since students often have difficulty dissociating a word and the specific context in which they first came across it. - Doze means to sleep lightly. What's the word? - I couldn't get comfortable on the train, so I only dozed. When you wake up a lot during the night, that's called dozing. - I'm going to name some activities. If you think you might sleep lightly during these activities, say doze. Otherwise, stay quiet. Ready? - Getting in your bed after a long day - On a bumpy airplane ride - Watching a boring movie - On a long car ride - During school - Hover means to hang in the air, like flying but not moving forward. What's the word? - A helicopter can hover and fly forward and backward. If you stand over someone to see what they're doing, it's called hovering. - I'm going to name some animals. If you think the animal can hang in the air, say hover. Otherwise, sit quietly. Ready? - A hummingbird - A mosquito - A hawk - A penguin - A lizard - Plunge means to jump down or dive. What's the word? - The seal plunged in the ocean to catch a fish. When you jump into the swimming pool, that's called plunging. - I'm going to name some things. If you think you'd jump in to that thing, say plunge. Otherwise, stay quiet. Ready? - A calm lake - A rushing river - A warm bathtub - A puddle of mud - A swimming pool - Scatter means to run or move quickly in every direction. What's the word? - The marbles scattered all over the kitchen when I dropped the bag. Cloud droplets are also about 1000 times heavier than evaporated water, so they are much heavier than air. They do not fall, but stay in the air, because there is warm air all round the heavier water droplets. When water changes from gas to droplets, this makes heat. Hugo began planning a major novel about social misery and injustice as early as the 1830s, but it would take a full 17 years for "Les Mis rables", to be realized and finally published in 1862. The author was acutely aware of the quality of the novel and publication of the work went to the highest bidder. The Belgian publishing house Lacroix and Verboeckhoven undertook a marketing campaign unusual for the time, issuing press releases about the work a full six months before the launch. It also initially published only the first part of the novel ("Fantine"), which was launched simultaneously in major cities. Installments of the book sold out within hours, and had enormous impact on French society. The critical establishment was generally hostile to the novel; Taine found it insincere, Barbey d'Aurevilly complained of its vulgarity, Flaubert found within it "neither truth nor greatness," the Goncourts lambasted its artificiality, and Baudelaire - despite giving favorable reviews in newspapers - castigated it in private as "tasteless and inept." "Les Mis rables" proved popular enough with the masses that the issues it highlighted were soon on the agenda of the French National Assembly. Today the novel remains his most enduringly popular work. It is popular worldwide, has been adapted for cinema, television and stage shows. You have to squeeze it and then pull it off. You can drink first and then baby a baby. In Spoken Mandarin, most words are character compounds because over time Mandarin lost many sounds that existed in earlier forms of Chinese. Due to the loss of sounds, many Chinese words ended up becoming homophones, thus more characters were added to words to tell them apart. For example, the Chinese title of the poem "Lion-Eating Poet in the Stone Den" is pronounced ""Sh Sh Sh Sh Sh "", and every character in the poem is pronounced with the same syllable, but with different tones. In older forms of Chinese, one could have been to tell apart the different characters making up the title, as they could have sounded noticeably different from each other. Title: How to Boot From a Drive Introduction: Booting your computer from a drive refers to starting up your operating system (OS) from that particular drive. This can be useful when you want to install a new OS or access files on a different drive. This tutorial will guide you through the process of booting from a drive, whether it's an internal hard drive, external hard drive, USB flash drive, or CD/DVD drive. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Backup important data before making any significant changes to your system configuration. 2. Be aware that changing boot order may affect other drives' ability to boot. 3. Make sure the target drive is properly connected and recognized by your computer. 4. Follow these steps exactly as described; deviating could result in unexpected issues. 5. Restart your computer multiple times during this process. Step 1: Access BIOS Setup To change the boot order, you need to enter the Basic Input Output System (BIOS) setup menu. To do so, restart your computer and immediately press the appropriate key depending on your manufacturer. Common keys include F1, F2, F10, ESC, or DEL. If uncertain, refer to your motherboard or laptop documentation. Step 2: Navigate to Boot Order Settings Once inside the BIOS setup menu, navigate using arrow keys to find 'Boot', 'Boot Order', 'Startup', or similar options. Each BIOS interface varies slightly, but they all serve the same purpose. Step 3: Change Boot Order Priority Within the boot settings, adjust the priority list to place the desired drive at the top. Typically, this involves highlighting the drive name then pressing + or - buttons to move its position up or down. Save changes once done. Step 4: Exit Saving Changes Exit out of the BIOS setup menu after saving changes. Your computer should automatically restart now. Step 5: Confirm Successful Boot Up Check if your computer successfully boots from the chosen drive. You can confirm this based on visual cues such as logos, loading screens, or background colors associated with specific OSes. For Specific Devices: USB Flash Drive/CD/DVD Drive: Ensure the device containing the OS installation media is inserted and set as first in the boot order. When restarting, the computer should load directly into the OS installation environment. Internal Hard Drive: If there are multiple internal hard drives, ensure the correct one is set as primary in the boot order. Upon restart, the computer should start up the OS installed on that drive. External Hard Drive: Connect the external hard drive via USB port and make sure it powers on correctly. Set it as first in the boot order following Steps 1-4 above. The computer must recognize the external drive as a valid boot option for this method to work. Conclusion: Changing boot order allows users to select which storage device their computer uses upon startup. By understanding how to modify boot priorities, individuals gain greater control over their systems and increase flexibility when managing multiple drives or testing new OS installations. Always remember to backup critical data prior to implementing major system modifications like altering boot orders. [6.7 sec silence, then S fusses twice] Achieving success in XCOM: UFO Defense requires strategic planning, quick decision-making, and careful resource management. As you progress through the game, avoiding common mistakes can significantly improve your chances of victory. Here is an in-depth guide on how to avoid these pitfalls: **Step 1: Proper Base Management** Start by building your base with care. Place critical facilities like the Power Generator, Living Quarters, and Workshop close together to minimize power and maintenance costs while maximizing efficiency. Remember that bases cannot be relocated once built, so plan accordingly! Additionally, ensure adequate funding from member nations; losing their support will cripple your operations. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep essential facilities close for cost optimization. - Don't forget about power distribution - uneven usage may lead to blackouts. - Maintain good relations with countries contributing funds. **Step 2: Effective Research Strategy** Prioritize research projects based on immediate needs rather than following a predetermined order. For example, studying Alien Alloys should come beforePsionics if you need better armor or weapons quickly. Also, remember that certain technologies unlock other paths, making them more valuable early on. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Prioritize urgent tech upgrades over less important ones. - Understand interdependencies between different technology branches. - Balance short-term gains with long-term benefits when choosing what to research next. </s> **Step 3: Efficient Use of Soldiers** Losing soldiers is inevitable but minimizing casualties is crucial. Equip your troops appropriately, focusing on high-damage weaponry, protective gear, and items enhancing accuracy or evasion. Deploy experienced soldiers whenever possible as they have higher stats and skills. Lastly, consider rotating veterans out of combat missions to preserve their longevity. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Equipped right saves lives: prioritize powerful weapons and defensive equipment. - Experience matters: favor seasoned soldiers who perform better under pressure. - Mindful deployment prevents unnecessary losses. **Step 4: Thoughtful Air Combat Tactics** Interceptors play a significant role against alien crafts. Optimize their loadout depending upon the threat level. Fightercraft are best suited for taking down Scouts, whereas Interceptors excel at destroying larger ships. Moreover, timely repair and maintenance of aircraft extend their lifespan. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Match interceptor type with appropriate threats. - Regular maintenance ensures peak performance and prolongs service life. - Strategic positioning (near potential hotspots) increases reaction time. **Step 5: Prudent Resource Allocation** Resources such as engineers, scientists, and money must be allocated wisely. Invest heavily in necessary areas such as manufacturing new equipment, conducting vital research, and expanding base infrastructure. However, don't overlook the importance of maintaining a strong defense force too. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Distribute resources where most needed: R&D, Manufacturing, or Base Expansion. - Never neglect soldier training and equipping. - Be prepared for unexpected situations - having reserves comes handy. Following these steps meticulously will help you navigate past typical blunders and pave the way towards triumph in XCOM: UFO Defense. Or if I baa like a sheep. Creating a Learner's Go Board Go, also known as Weiqi or Baduk, is an ancient board game originating from China over 2,500 years ago. It is a strategy-based game played by two players who take turns placing stones on a grid of intersecting lines. The objective is to control more territory than your opponent. This tutorial will guide you through creating a learner's Go board, which is a simplified version designed specifically for beginners to learn and understand the rules and strategies of the game. Materials needed: * A large piece of cardboard or foam core (at least 18" x 18") * Grid template (downloadable online) * Ruler * Pencil * Scissors or box cutter * Black and white paint or markers * Paintbrushes * Optional: Clear adhesive film or contact paper to protect the surface Step 1: Prepare Your Materials Gather all necessary materials before starting. Make sure the cardboard or foam core is clean, flat, and free of debris. If using foam core, be cautious when cutting as it can be fragile. Step 2: Create the Grid Template Download a 9x9 or 13x13 grid template suitable for a beginner's Go board. These dimensions are smaller than a standard full-size board but still provide enough space for learning the basics. Print out the template and carefully cut along the edges to create a sturdy outline. Step 3: Trace the Grid onto the Board Place the grid template centered on the board. Use a ruler to ensure straight alignment. Lightly trace around the edge of the template with a pencil. Remove the template once traced. Step 4: Draw Intersection Points Using the ruler, draw perpendicular lines connecting opposite sides of the squares within the traced grid. This should result in small dots at every intersection point where stones will be placed during play. Erase any unnecessary pencil marks. Step 5: Apply Paint or Markers Carefully fill in alternate squares with black and white paint or markers. For example, start with black then switch to white for every other square. Allow ample time for the paint to dry completely before proceeding to the next step. Step 6: Protect the Surface (Optional) To prevent wear and tear on your new Go board, consider applying clear adhesive film or contact paper over the painted surface. Cut the film or paper slightly larger than the board and smooth it down firmly, ensuring there are no air bubbles trapped underneath. Trim excess material from the edges. Step 7: Gather Stones You can purchase Go stones made of various materials such as plastic, glass, or stone online or at specialty game stores. Alternatively, you can make DIY stones using coins, buttons, or even bottle caps. Ideally, have at least 36 stones in both colors - enough for each player to cover their half of the board if necessary. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Choose high contrast colors for easy visibility during play. * Double check measurements and alignments throughout the process to maintain accuracy. * Consider laminating the board for added durability. * Always store the board and stones properly after use to avoid damage. * Encourage proper etiquette during games, including keeping hands off opponents' stones and refraining from distractions while playing. By following these steps and guidelines, you will create a functional and educational tool that allows beginners to learn and enjoy the fascinating world of Go! Once upon a time there was a pink spear. It was so shiny that it shone like a sparkly star! One day a little girl was playing with it. She loved her pink spear very much and wanted to show it to the world. So, the girl decided to send her pink spear on a special journey. She sent it far away, over the hills and far away. As you may know, the stars are so far away from us that the patterns they make in the sky stay the same. However a few of the bright specs we see from earth seem to move around. In ancient times these wandering stars were known as the planets which meant "the wanderers". These days we've found out that they are the other worlds that share our solar system and go round our sun. The nearest star is about 4,000 times further away than the distant dwarf planet Pluto. When a planet is on the other side of the sun from us we can't see it, but at other times it can appear. How to use the sky map The Gobsmacking Galaxy What can you see in the sky? What are the other planets really like? How do you travel in space? How could your children become older than you? Where do stars come from? What are red giants and black holes... ...the answers are all in here! Available in over 25 languages! Set the time and date you want to look at the sky. - Set where you are on the earth. You can use latitude and longitude co-ordinates if you know them. (Whole numbers only please - or the programme gets confused!) Otherwise there's a drop down list of major cities, so pick the nearest one to you. - The planets in the sky will appear along with the moon! Note: the map has SOUTH to the left and NORTH to the right. The number after the planet is its apparent magnitude, which is a measure of how bright it is. It's a complicated logarithmic scale, but the main thing to know is that a lower number is brighter and so negative numbers are very bright! Here are a few magnitudes: Sirius, the brightest star faintest things you can see in a normal sky over a town faintest things you can see in a perfect dark sky faintest things you can see with binoculars * These are the maximum brightnesses of each planet. Mars looks good here, but actually it's very rare for it to be brighter than Jupiter! MERCURY is nearest the sun. Although it is always in the sky during daylight hours, it can only be seen just before sun rise or just after sun set, and is quite hard to spot unless you know what you're looking for. (Do NOT stare at the sun!) VENUS is the only other planet in between us and the sun. After the sun and the moon, it is the brightest object in the sky. It can appear very brightly either at dusk or dawn and the ancients used to think it was two different stars - the "evening star" or the "morning star". Mercury and Venus are the inferior planets which means they orbit between us and the sun so we can never see them in the middle of the night. These two change their positions quite noticeably from one night to the next. All the other planets are superior, which means that they can be on the opposite side of Earth from the sun and so they can appear at midnight and look their best. MARS takes nearly twice as long as we do to go around the sun, which means that we get the best views roughly once every two years. It's distinctly orange in colour. JUPITER If you see a very bright object in the middle of the night, it can't be Venus, so it will usually be Jupiter. If you have good binoculars you might even be able to see up to four tiny bright dots next to it. These are Jupiter's four major moons! SATURN looks like quite a bright star and with a decent telescope you might see the rings! For thousands of years people regarded 7 as a magic number because of the seven wonders of the sky which were the sun and moon and the five planets they could see. But then in 1781 URANUS was discovered, then in 1846 they found NEPTUNE and in 1930 PLUTO. Sadly though, most of us are just like the ancient people and we'll never get to see the last three just using our eyes! To the Murderous Maths Home page THANKS! In the case of line segments, the cone does not extend beyond the base, while in the case of half-lines, it extends infinitely far. In the case of lines, the cone extends infinitely far in both directions from the apex, in which case it is sometimes called a double cone. Either half of a double cone on one side of the apex is called a nappe. " Mommy sheep is called a ewe and daddy is a ram they both have wooly coats just like their baby lamb! " Among the most brilliant and most rare objects in the night sky. These soaring beacons with their beautiful tails come from the outer realms of the Solar System. What are comets? A comet is a small world which scientists sometimes call a planetesimal. They are made out of dust and ice, kind of like a dirty snow ball. Where do they come from? Comets come from two places: The Kuiper Belt and the Oort Cloud. Many people think that a comet's tail is always following behind it, but actually the coma, or tail, can either be behind the comet or in front of it. Which way the tail is pointing depends on where the Sun is. That's right, the Sun's heat and radiation produce a wind called the Solar Wind, as a comet gets close to the Sun it begins to melt. The gas and dust that melt off are blown away from the Sun by the solar winds. So if a comet is traveling towards the Sun then the tail will follow behind, but if the comet is traveling away from the Sun the tail will be in front of the comet. Imagine a place far, far away at the very edge of the Solar System. A place where millions of comets can be seen swishing around in every direction. These icy comets are orbiting the Sun in two different places, both of which are very distant. One place is called the Oort cloud, and the other is called the Kuiper Belt. Why do Comets leave their home in the Oort Cloud or Kuiper Belt? A comet will spend billions of years in the Kuiper Belt or Oort Cloud. Sometimes two comets will come very close to each other, or even crash into one another. When this happens the comets change directions. Sometimes their new path will bring them into the Inner Solar System. This is when a comet begins to shine. Up until now the comet has been among millions of others exactly the same, but as they approach the warmer Inner Solar System they begin to melt leaving behind magnificent tails. Unfortunately, comets don't live very long once they enter the warmer part of the Solar System. Just like a snowman melts in the summer, comets melt in the Inner Solar System. Although it is the most glorious part of their lives, traveling through the Inner Solar System eventually kills them. After several thousand years they melt down to a little bit of ice and dust, not nearly enough to leave a tail. Some even melt away completely. Would it be safe to fly through the tail of a comet? Unlike a recent blockbuster movie showing a space ship flying past giant rocks the size of houses, a comet's tail is actually quite safe. The only thing that would hit your ship would be microscopic pieces of dust. Would you like to play with the animals? Title: How to Care for a Belly Button Ring Introduction: Getting a belly button ring can be an exciting experience, but it's important to remember that proper care is crucial to prevent infection and ensure healing. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to care for your new belly button piercing. Step 1: Clean Your Hands Before touching or handling your belly button ring, wash your hands thoroughly with antibacterial soap and warm water. Dry them completely using a clean towel to avoid introducing bacteria into the piercing site. This prevents contamination and reduces the risk of infection. Step 2: Initial Piercing Care After getting your navel pierced, keep the original jewelry in place at all times during the first 4-6 weeks while it heals. Twist the jewelry gently once or twice a day to ensure the tunnel doesn't close up or grow over the jewelry. The initial jewelry should have a longer post to accommodate swelling. Key Tip: Avoid removing the jewelry entirely during this period as doing so could cause the hole to close. Step 3: Use Saline Solution Use a sterile saline solution (0.9% sodium chloride) to clean your piercing. Soak a cotton ball or gauze pad in the solution and hold it against the piercing for about five minutes. Repeat this process two to three times daily for the first few weeks after piercing. Saline solutions help promote healing by keeping the area free from debris and reducing irritation. Step 4: Rinse After Showering Rinse your piercing well after taking showers or baths to remove any residual soap or shampoo which might contain harsh chemicals that could irritate the wound. Step 5: Avoid Touching Unnecessarily Refrain from needlessly fiddling with your new piercing. Every time you touch it, there's potential for germ transfer which may lead to infection. If you must adjust the jewelry, make sure your hands are freshly washed. Step 6: Maintain Good Personal Hygiene Maintaining good personal hygiene is vital when caring for a belly button ring. Wear loose clothing made of breathable fabrics like cotton to minimize friction and allow air circulation around the piercing site. Also, maintain clean bed sheets and towels to avoid reintroducing bacteria. Step 7: Stay Away From Certain Activities During the healing phase, abstain from swimming pools, hot tubs, saunas, and ocean waters due to high bacterial content. Refrain from applying lotions, creams, or oils near the piercing as they can trap bacteria and hinder healing. Step 8: Regular Check-ups Regularly check your piercing for signs of infection such as redness, tenderness, discharge, or fever. Should these symptoms occur, consult your piercer or healthcare provider immediately. Step 9: Changing Jewelry Wait until your piercing has fully healed before changing the jewelry - typically six months to one year. When ready, replace the old piece slowly and carefully to avoid tearing the tissue. Always clean the new jewelry before insertion. Conclusion: Proper care of a belly button ring involves regular cleaning, patience, and attentiveness. By following these steps diligently, you significantly reduce the chances of complications and ensure a smooth healing process. Remember, if anything seems amiss, seek professional advice promptly. Okay, before I even begin this lesson, I want to say something: The method that you're about to learn for making higher numbers should be avoided as much as possible. While it is possible to say numbers like 23 in Toki Pona, by no means should you specify these particular numbers unless it is crucial that you do so. Okay, now as you've probably already noticed, there are only two number words in Toki Pona: wan (1) and tu (2). Additionally, ala can be used to mean 0. When we need to make higher numbers, we combine these numbers together. Essentially, you just string wan and tu together to add up to the number you want. For example, here's how you add tu and wan together to make 3: tu wan = 2 + 1 = 3 Neat, huh? Here are some more examples: tu tu = 2 + 2 = 4 tu tu tu wan = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 These numbers are added onto nouns just like adjectives: jan tu wan = 3 people jan lili tu tu = 4 children As you can see, expressing higher numbers quickly becomes tedious. Toki Pona was designed this way intentionally to encourage you to focus on simplicity. Use mute. Conserve the Numbers. Okay, so it's a bad idea to use the numbers when you don't absolutely need them. So, instead, we use mute for any number higher than two: jan mute li kama. -- Many people came. Of course, this is still pretty vague. mute in the above sentence could mean 3 or it could mean 3 000. Fortunately, mute is just an adjective, and so we can attach other adjectives after it. Here's what you might say if there were lots and lots of people: jan mute mute mute li kama! -- Many, many, many people are coming! More than likely, that sentence is saying that at least a thousand people are coming. Now suppose that you had more than two people but still not very many. Let's say that the number is around 4 or 5. Here's how you'd say that: jan mute lili li kama. -- A small amount (of) people are coming. Once again, these descriptions are still relative and would change depending on what you're talking about. Just try to use good judgment, and remember that Toki Pona is not about being exact. If you understood how the cardinal numbers work, the ordinal numbers only require one more step. Here's how you'd translate "4th person": jan pi nanpa tu tu -- person of number 4; 4th person Like I said, if you understood the cardinal numbers, it's easy because you just stick pi nanpa in between the noun and the number. Here are a few more examples, if you think you need to look at them: ni li jan lili ona pi nanpa tu. -- This is her second child. meli mi pi nanpa wan li nasa. -- My first girlfriend was crazy. Other Uses of wan and tu wan can be used as a verb. It means to unite. mi en meli mi li wan. -- I and my girlfriend united. My girlfriend and I got married. tu used as a verb means to split or to divide. o tu e palisa ni. -- Split this stick. Break it into two pieces. A Note about luka The word luka used to mean 5. Its use as a number was very similar to what I described above for wan and tu; for example, luka tu would have been how to say 7. This use of luka was removed to keep the language's focus on simplicity. I am mentioning this former meaning of luka here because you may see it in some older texts, and I want you to be familiar with the meaning. Today's word is weka. As a verb, it just means to get rid of, to remove, etc. o weka e len sina. -- Remove your clothes. o weka e jan lili tan ni. ona li wile ala kute e ni. -- Remove the kid from here. He shouldn't hear this. weka is also used very often as an adjective and an adverb. mi weka. -- I was away. mi wile tawa weka. -- I want to go away. I want to leave. It can also be used to mean the equivalent of far or distant. tomo mi li weka tan ni. -- My house is away from here. ma Elopa li weka tan ma Mewika. -- Europe is away from the USA. And add ala to mean that it's somewhere closeby: ma Mewika li weka ala tan ma Kupa. -- The USA is not away from Cuba. Try changing these sentences from English into Toki Pona. I saw three birds. Many people are coming. The first person is here. I own two cars. Some (but not a lot) of people are coming. And now try changing these sentences from Toki Pona into English: mi weka e ijo tu ni. mi lukin e soweli luka. I think perhaps he was going to put it up down there, wasn't he? [the /n/ is separated from the rest; 6.7 sec, rustles] Cleaning vinyl flooring may seem like a simple task, but there are many steps you can take to ensure that your floors are as clean and shiny as possible. By following this detailed guide, you'll be able to effectively remove dirt, grime, and stains from your vinyl floors, leaving them looking like new. **Things You'll Need:** * Broom or vacuum cleaner with a hardfloor attachment * Microfiber mop or sponge mop * Bucket * Hot water * Mild dish soap or vinegar * Soft cloths or towels * Dry mop or towel **Step 1: Sweep or Vacuum Your Floors** The first step in cleaning vinyl flooring is to sweep or vacuum up any loose dirt or debris. This will make the rest of the cleaning process easier and more effective. Be sure to get into corners and along edges where dust and crumbs can accumulate. If using a vacuum cleaner, make sure it has a hardfloor setting to avoid scratching the vinyl. **Step 2: Prepare Your Cleaning Solution** Fill a bucket with hot water. Add a few drops of mild dish soap or cup of white vinegar per gallon of water. Both options are great at cutting through grease and grime without harming the vinyl surface. Stir the solution gently to mix. **Step 3: Damp-Mop the Floor** Dip your microfiber mop or sponge mop into the cleaning solution, wring it out so it's damp but not dripping wet, then begin mopping the floor. Work in small sections, going back over each section after washing to ensure all dirt and soap residue is removed. Rinse the mop frequently in the cleaning solution to prevent reapplying dirt. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Avoid using too much water, which could seep under the vinyl causing damage or warping. * Don't use abrasive scrubbers or harsh chemicals, as they can scratch or discolor the vinyl. **Step 4: Spot Clean Tough Stains** If you come across tough stains while mopping, create a paste made from baking soda and warm water. Apply the paste directly onto the stain, let sit for about five minutes, then gently scrub with a soft cloth until the stain disappears. Wipe away excess paste with a damp cloth. **Step 5: Dry the Floor** After mopping, immediately dry the floor using a dry mop or towel to prevent water spots and slipping hazards. Make sure the floor is completely dry before walking on it. **Step 6: Regular Maintenance** Regularly sweeping or vacuuming your vinyl floors will help keep them looking their best between deep cleans. For high traffic areas, consider daily maintenance. Also place doormats at entrances to trap outside dirt. By following these steps, you'll have beautifully cleaned vinyl floors! Remember, regular care and maintenance will extend the life of your floors and reduce the frequency of deep cleans. A webpage is one page of a website. Every web page has a web address. When children die from diarrhea, the cause is often dehydration (losing too much water from the body). Because diarrhea is watery, it takes away a lot of the water. It also takes away electrolytes-important salts that the body needs to survive. Dehydration is extra dangerous for small children because they have less water in their bodies to begin with. This means they cannot lose as much water as adults before they start to have serious health problems. oh, you going to try and get that cone off the ice cream. What do you do in this house?
